QuestionText: The following statement is correct: Event Source → Trigger Layer → Devin Session → Review Gate
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: In Devin's SDLC integration model, the Devin API endpoint for creating sessions is invoked via POST /sessions from the trigger layer.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Which of the following are recognized event sources that can trigger a Devin session? Select all that apply.
Option1: CI pipeline failure
Option2: SAST finding above threshold
Option3: Work item tagged in an issue tracker
Option4: Schedule (cron expression)

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: To prevent infinite automation loops where Devin's output triggers itself repeatedly
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: To prevent duplicate sessions from being created when the same event is delivered multiple times
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: The webhook payload shape and findings format that any tool in that category must produce
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: So Devin's isolated VM can run the full build/test cycle without requiring access to external shared infrastructure
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The PR feedback loop design pattern ensures that Devin never merges its own pull requests — the merge decision always belongs to a human reviewer.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: A client wants to connect their SonarQube instance to trigger Devin sessions for automatic remediation of HIGH findings. Which integration architecture should the architect implement?
Option1: Configure SonarQube webhook → intermediary trigger layer (e.g., GitHub Actions or Lambda) → POST to Devin API /sessions with finding details in the prompt
Option2: Install SonarQube directly on Devin's VM and have it scan during every session
Option3: Poll the SonarQube API every 5 minutes from a scheduled Devin session
Option4: Embed SonarQube credentials in the Devin prompt so it can query findings on demand

QuestionText: An architect is designing a CI failure auto-remediation pipeline. The CI system is Azure DevOps Pipelines. Which trigger mechanism is MOST appropriate?
Option1: Azure DevOps service hook (webhook) on build failure → Azure Function → Devin API POST /sessions with pipeline run ID and failure logs
Option2: Devin polls the Azure DevOps API every minute to check for failed builds
Option3: Add a final step in every pipeline YAML that calls Devin if the previous step failed
Option4: Configure Devin as a required reviewer on all PRs so it monitors build results natively

QuestionText: A client's development workflow requires that every Jira ticket tagged 'Devin:Implementation' triggers an automated session. The architect must prevent the same ticket from spawning multiple sessions if Jira sends duplicate webhooks. Which safeguard applies?
Option1: Include the Jira issue key as an idempotency key in the Devin API call so duplicate events for the same ticket are rejected
Option2: Set a rate limit on the Devin API of one session per minute
Option3: Configure Jira to only send webhooks during business hours
Option4: Have Devin check if a branch already exists for that ticket before starting work

QuestionText: A team wants Devin to handle weekly dependency updates across 12 microservices. Each service is independent. What is the correct SDLC integration pattern?
Option1: Scheduled parent session (weekly cron) that spawns 12 child sessions — one per service — each running the same dependency update playbook
Option2: 12 separate cron schedules, each pointing to a different repo with the same prompt
Option3: A single scheduled session that clones all 12 repos sequentially and opens PRs
Option4: A GitHub Actions workflow that runs npm update in all repos and commits directly to main

QuestionText: An architect is implementing a security scanning pipeline using the toolchain-agnostic stub pattern. The client currently uses Checkmarx but plans to migrate to Snyk within 6 months. How should the trigger layer be designed?
Option1: Define a normalized findings schema that both Checkmarx and Snyk webhooks are transformed into before invoking the Devin API — the Devin prompt references the normalized format, not the tool-specific one
Option2: Build two separate trigger pipelines, one for Checkmarx and one for Snyk, each with its own Devin prompt
Option3: Wait until the Snyk migration is complete before building the automation
Option4: Have Devin install both Checkmarx and Snyk CLI tools on its VM and run whichever is configured

QuestionText: A client wants to integrate Devin into their Planning phase of the SDLC for technical research. Which pattern is MOST appropriate?
Option1: Use Ask Devin or single-agent sessions with research-oriented prompts that produce markdown reports analyzing codebases, dependencies, or architecture options
Option2: Configure event-driven triggers from the project management tool to spawn Devin sessions for every new epic
Option3: Set up a parent-child campaign to parallelize research across all planned features
Option4: Have Devin join sprint planning meetings via calendar integration

QuestionText: An architect must design a multi-phase automation pipeline: (1) nightly SAST scan detects findings, (2) findings are triaged by severity, (3) HIGH/CRITICAL findings trigger immediate remediation sessions, (4) MEDIUM findings are batched for weekly remediation. Which design correctly implements this?
Option1: Scheduled nightly session runs scan → webhook on completion posts findings to a triage Lambda → Lambda routes HIGHs immediately to Devin API and queues MEDIUMs → weekly cron drains the MEDIUM queue to batch Devin sessions
Option2: Single nightly Devin session that scans, triages, and remediates all findings sequentially in one long-running session
Option3: Two independent schedules: one nightly for HIGH remediation, one weekly for MEDIUM — both run their own scans
Option4: A parent session that spawns a child for every finding regardless of severity, with the children self-triaging

QuestionText: A client's SDLC includes a manual deployment approval gate. They want Devin to prepare the deployment artifact and IaC changes, but NOT execute the deployment. How should the architect design this integration?
Option1: Devin session generates IaC changes and deployment manifest in a PR → human approves and merges → existing CD pipeline (not Devin) executes the deployment
Option2: Devin applies the IaC changes to a staging environment, then waits for human approval before applying to production
Option3: Devin creates a separate deployment session that hibernates until the human triggers it via API
Option4: Configure Devin with deployment credentials but add a Knowledge note saying 'never deploy without asking'

QuestionText: A client's CI pipeline takes 45 minutes to complete. When Devin opens a PR and CI fails, the feedback loop is nearly an hour. The architect needs to reduce iteration time. Which approach is MOST architecturally sound?
Option1: Configure the environment blueprint to run the relevant test subset locally before pushing, so CI failures are caught in seconds rather than waiting for the full pipeline
Option2: Parallelize all CI jobs to reduce the pipeline from 45 to 15 minutes
Option3: Have Devin push directly to main to skip the PR CI check
Option4: Disable CI checks for Devin-created PRs and rely on local test results only

QuestionText: A development team has both a staging environment and a production environment. Devin is triggered by a SAST finding in production code. The architect must decide where Devin should validate its fix. Which analysis is CORRECT?
Option1: Devin should validate locally on its VM (unit tests, lint, SAST re-scan) and via CI on the PR branch — never directly against production or staging, as the PR merge + CD pipeline handles deployment
Option2: Devin should deploy the fix to staging first to validate against production-like data
Option3: Devin should run integration tests against the production environment to ensure the fix works in real conditions
Option4: Devin should validate only in CI since local tests cannot replicate the production finding

QuestionText: An automation triggers a Devin session whenever a PR is opened by a developer. Devin reviews the PR and may open a fix PR if it finds bugs. A colleague points out this could create a loop: Devin's fix PR triggers another review session. What is the ROOT CAUSE of this risk?
Option1: The automation rule does not filter events by PR author — it treats Devin's own PRs the same as developer PRs, creating a recursive trigger
Option2: Devin Review is configured to open fix PRs instead of just commenting
Option3: The trigger fires on PR opened events instead of PR merged events
Option4: The webhook endpoint does not validate the event signature

QuestionText: A client wants Devin integrated into every SDLC phase. The architect proposes: Planning (research sessions), Implementation (coding sessions), Testing (test generation), Deployment (IaC PRs), Operations (scheduled maintenance). A reviewer argues this over-automates the Design phase. Which analysis BEST evaluates this concern?
Option1: The concern is valid — the proposal correctly excludes novel architecture design from automation because it requires subjective human judgment that cannot be verified by tests; research assistance in Planning is appropriate but Design decisions remain human
Option2: The concern is invalid — Devin can design systems as effectively as humans since it has access to all code and documentation
Option3: The concern is partially valid — Devin should do the design but present three options for humans to choose from
Option4: The concern is invalid because the proposal already excludes Design by only listing Planning, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Operations

QuestionText: An architect is evaluating two trigger architectures for CI failure remediation: (A) Direct webhook from CI to Devin API, (B) CI webhook → intermediary Lambda → Devin API. Which analysis of option B's advantages is MOST accurate?
Option1: The intermediary allows filtering (ignore flaky tests), enrichment (attach relevant logs), rate limiting (max retries), and format normalization — all without modifying the CI system or Devin's prompt structure
Option2: The intermediary adds unnecessary latency and a single point of failure without meaningful benefit
Option3: The intermediary is only needed if the CI system cannot make HTTPS calls directly
Option4: The intermediary is required because the Devin API does not accept webhook payloads

QuestionText: A team has configured Devin to respond to incident alerts. After deployment, they notice that a single flapping alert (firing and resolving repeatedly) spawns dozens of Devin sessions. Which safeguard was missing from the architecture?
Option1: A maximum retry/session count per trigger source within a time window, combined with deduplication logic that groups rapid-fire alerts for the same incident
Option2: A Knowledge note telling Devin to ignore repeated alerts
Option3: A longer hibernation timeout so Devin does not respond too quickly
Option4: Alert suppression at the monitoring tool level that prevents all alerts during business hours

QuestionText: A client's SDLC has a mandatory code review requirement: all changes must be reviewed by at least two human engineers before merge. An architect proposes adding Devin Review as one of the two required reviewers. Which analysis of this proposal is MOST accurate?
Option1: Devin Review should supplement but not replace a human reviewer count — it catches different classes of issues (logic errors, security) but cannot evaluate architectural fit, team conventions, or business context the way a human reviewer can
Option2: Devin Review is superior to human review and should count as both required reviews since it is more consistent
Option3: Devin Review should never be part of the required review process because it is not a human
Option4: Devin Review can replace one human reviewer only for PRs under 100 lines of changes

QuestionText: An architect notices that a client's automation creates a Devin session for every single Jira ticket transition, including status changes like 'In Review' → 'Done'. Most of these sessions produce no useful output. What is the architectural problem?
Option1: The trigger filter is too broad — it should only fire on specific transitions (e.g., 'To Do' → 'In Progress' with a 'Devin:Implementation' label) rather than all status changes
Option2: Devin should be configured to recognize irrelevant triggers and self-terminate without action
Option3: The Jira webhook should be replaced with a polling mechanism that checks every hour
Option4: The automation should be disabled and replaced with manual session creation only

QuestionText: Two integration patterns are proposed for a client: (A) Devin as a CI step that runs after tests pass — it generates documentation and opens a separate PR; (B) Devin triggered by PR merge event — it generates documentation in a follow-up PR. Which analysis of the trade-offs is CORRECT?
Option1: Pattern B is superior because it decouples documentation from the development PR lifecycle, avoids blocking CI completion, and triggers only on merged code (confirmed changes) rather than potentially abandoned PRs
Option2: Pattern A is superior because it runs documentation generation earlier, before code reaches main
Option3: Both patterns are equivalent since documentation is generated from the same code regardless of when it triggers
Option4: Pattern A is superior because it ensures documentation is always in sync with the PR that generated it

QuestionText: A client has a monorepo with 15 services. They want Devin to only run tests relevant to the changed service, not the full test suite. The CI takes 90 minutes for the full suite. Which architectural decision BEST addresses this?
Option1: Configure the environment blueprint to include a test-selection script that determines affected services from the git diff and runs only those tests locally — Devin executes this before pushing, catching most issues in seconds
Option2: Have Devin always run the full test suite to ensure no cross-service regressions
Option3: Split the monorepo into 15 separate repositories so each CI pipeline is faster
Option4: Configure CI to run all 15 service tests in parallel on the PR branch after Devin pushes

QuestionText: A client wants to automate their entire bug-fix workflow: bug report submitted → Devin implements fix → PR opened → CI passes → auto-merge. An architect objects to the final step. Which analysis BEST supports the objection?
Option1: Auto-merge violates the principle that the merge decision always belongs to a human — the PR is the review gate and audit point, and removing human approval eliminates the safety mechanism that prevents incorrect or incomplete fixes from reaching production
Option2: Auto-merge is fine for bug fixes since they have clear acceptance criteria (tests pass)
Option3: The objection is about ACU cost, not safety — auto-merge means Devin does not hibernate while waiting for review
Option4: Auto-merge is acceptable only if Devin Review is configured as an additional check

QuestionText: A client asks why they should use Devin's event-driven pattern instead of a simple cron job that runs a remediation script. Which analysis BEST differentiates the two approaches?
Option1: A cron job runs the same commands every time regardless of context. Devin's intelligent agent reads context, makes decisions about implementation approach, adapts when things fail, verifies its work through tests and CI, and communicates results via PRs — the same workflow a human engineer follows
Option2: There is no meaningful difference — both execute code on a schedule
Option3: Devin is more expensive and therefore only justified for complex tasks
Option4: A cron job cannot run on a schedule while Devin can

QuestionText: An architect is designing webhook topology for a client with 3 SAST tools, 2 CI systems, and 4 issue trackers. A junior architect proposes building a dedicated integration for each tool-to-Devin connection (3+2+4 = 9 integrations). What is the fundamental design flaw?
Option1: It violates the toolchain-agnostic stub pattern — the architect should define normalized interface contracts per category (security findings, build failures, work items) and build 3 category adapters that transform any tool's output into the standard format, reducing 9 integrations to 3 canonical trigger paths
Option2: Nine integrations is too many for one person to maintain — the team should prioritize the most important 3
Option3: Each integration needs its own Lambda function which increases cloud costs
Option4: The junior architect should build a single universal adapter that handles all 9 tools identically

QuestionText: A client reports that Devin sessions triggered by their alerting system often fail because the alert does not contain enough context for Devin to diagnose the issue. Which architectural improvement addresses this?
Option1: Add an enrichment step in the trigger layer that queries the monitoring system for recent logs, metrics, and traces related to the alert, then includes this context in the Devin session prompt
Option2: Configure Devin with more Knowledge notes about common alert types
Option3: Increase the session timeout so Devin has more time to investigate
Option4: Route all alerts through a human triage step before invoking Devin

QuestionText: A financial services client needs an end-to-end SDLC integration: ticket assignment triggers implementation, PR opens automatically, Devin Review provides first-pass review, human reviews second, CI must pass including security scans, and the merged code triggers deployment to staging. Design the complete trigger chain.
Option1: Jira webhook (ticket tagged) → trigger Lambda → Devin API (implementation session) → PR opened → Devin Review auto-triggered → human review → CI (tests + SAST) passes → merge → CD webhook → staging deployment pipeline executes independently
Option2: Jira webhook → Devin implements and deploys to staging in one session → human reviews in staging → PR is retroactively opened for audit
Option3: Scheduled nightly session reads all new tickets → implements in batch → opens PRs → auto-merges if CI passes → deploys to staging
Option4: Human manually creates Devin session per ticket → Devin implements → human deploys after review

QuestionText: Design an automation architecture where: (1) A developer opens a PR, (2) Devin Review identifies potential bugs, (3) If bugs are found, a remediation session automatically opens a fix PR targeting the developer's branch, (4) The developer sees both the review comments and the fix PR. Include safeguards.
Option1: PR opened event → Devin Review runs → if findings exist: automation creates a remediation session targeting the developer's branch (not main) → Devin opens fix PR into the developer's branch → safeguards: filter out PRs authored by devin-ai-integration[bot], max 1 remediation per source PR, idempotency key = source PR number
Option2: PR opened → Devin Review → Review comments trigger a new event → Devin session reads comments and pushes commits to the same PR → no separate fix PR needed
Option3: PR opened → Devin clones the PR branch → rewrites the entire branch → force pushes the rewritten branch → developer sees updated PR
Option4: PR opened → Devin Review → if bugs found, close the original PR and open a new corrected PR → developer reviews the replacement

QuestionText: A client wants a progressive automation maturity path: Phase 1 (manual triggers only), Phase 2 (event-driven for security), Phase 3 (full SDLC integration with scheduled maintenance). Design the Phase 2 architecture that builds toward Phase 3.
Option1: Phase 2: SAST webhook → normalized findings adapter (toolchain-agnostic stub) → Devin API with idempotency + retry limits → PR with remediation → human review. Build the adapter pattern and safeguards now so Phase 3 reuses the same trigger infrastructure for additional event sources (CI, tickets, schedules)
Option2: Phase 2: Install SAST CLI on Devin VM → scheduled session runs scan and fixes everything → no webhook needed. Rebuild completely for Phase 3
Option3: Phase 2: SAST emails findings to a shared inbox → manual forward to Devin → gradual automation later
Option4: Phase 2: Build all Phase 3 infrastructure immediately but only enable the security triggers — the cost is the same either way

QuestionText: Design a feedback loop architecture where Devin's PR quality improves over time: engineer reviews identify common issues → those patterns are codified → future sessions automatically avoid those patterns. What components are needed?
Option1: PR review comments are analyzed periodically → recurring patterns are extracted and added as Knowledge notes with appropriate scope/triggers → Devin automatically retrieves relevant Knowledge notes in future sessions → the context layer compounds quality over time
Option2: Configure Devin Review to watch for the same patterns human reviewers flag and auto-reject PRs that contain them
Option3: Build a custom ML model trained on PR review history that pre-screens Devin's code before it creates PRs
Option4: Have a senior engineer manually update the Devin prompt template every sprint with new rules

QuestionText: A client needs Devin integrated into their incident management workflow: alert fires → Devin investigates → produces root cause analysis → if fix is safe, opens PR → if fix is risky, escalates to on-call with analysis. Design the decision architecture.
Option1: Alert webhook → enrichment layer (attach logs/metrics via MCP) → Devin session prompt includes investigation + classification criteria → Devin's session uses connected observability tools to investigate → if diagnosis yields a low-risk code fix (test-verifiable), opens PR → if high-risk or infrastructure-level, posts analysis to incident channel via Slack/Teams MCP and escalates to human
Option2: Alert → Devin always opens a fix PR regardless of risk → human decides whether to merge or reject
Option3: Alert → human on-call triages and decides whether to invoke Devin → Devin only implements if explicitly told to
Option4: Alert → Devin attempts fix → if CI fails 3 times, posts to Slack that it cannot resolve the issue

QuestionText: Design a multi-repo SDLC integration where changes to a shared library automatically trigger dependent service validation: shared lib PR merges → all consuming services are tested against the new version → incompatibilities are auto-remediated.
Option1: Shared lib merge event → webhook triggers parent Devin session → parent identifies all dependent services from a dependency manifest → spawns child sessions per service (bump version, run tests) → children that encounter breaking changes attempt auto-remediation → children open PRs → parent monitors and reports aggregate status
Option2: Configure CI on each dependent repo to trigger when the shared lib repo has a new commit → CI failures email the team → team manually asks Devin to fix
Option3: Shared lib PR includes changes to all dependent services in the same PR → monolithic approach ensures everything is validated together
Option4: Schedule a weekly job that bumps the shared lib version across all services simultaneously

QuestionText: A client with a microservices architecture wants Devin to manage their entire Operations SDLC phase: dependency monitoring, dead code detection, documentation freshness, and incident response. Design the scheduling and trigger topology.
Option1: Four automation configurations: (1) Weekly cron → parent session for dependency updates across all services (child per service), (2) Monthly cron → dead code analysis per service, (3) PR merge event → documentation refresh session for the changed service, (4) Alert webhook → incident investigation session with MCP access to observability tools — each with appropriate safeguards (idempotency, retry limits, author filtering)
Option2: One daily mega-session that sequentially performs all four tasks across all services
Option3: Four separate Devin organizations, one per operational concern, to maintain separation
Option4: A single always-running Devin session that monitors all systems continuously

QuestionText: A client needs a deployment verification pattern: after CD deploys to staging, Devin should run smoke tests and either approve or raise an issue. If approved, the CD pipeline should proceed to production. Design this integration.
Option1: CD staging deployment completion → webhook → Devin session runs smoke test suite against staging endpoint → if pass: Devin calls CD API to approve production gate (or updates a status check) → if fail: Devin opens a GitHub issue with failure details and blocks the pipeline gate
Option2: Devin deploys to staging itself, then runs tests, then deploys to production if tests pass — all in one session
Option3: CD pipeline includes Devin as a step that runs inline within the pipeline executor
Option4: Scheduled session checks staging health every 5 minutes and files issues when something is wrong

QuestionText: Design a test generation strategy integrated into the SDLC: whenever a developer opens a PR without adequate test coverage, Devin automatically generates tests for the new code and opens a companion PR.
Option1: PR opened event → coverage analysis job (CI step or standalone) calculates coverage delta → if below threshold: automation triggers Devin session with the diff and target coverage → Devin generates tests following existing patterns → opens companion PR targeting the developer's branch → author filter prevents triggering on Devin's own companion PRs
Option2: Devin Review comments on the PR requesting the developer add tests → if ignored for 24 hours, Devin generates them
Option3: Pre-commit hook prevents pushing without 80% coverage → developer must write tests locally before Devin can review
Option4: Weekly scheduled session scans all PRs merged without tests and retroactively adds test PRs against main

QuestionText: A regulated client requires that every automated code change includes traceability: the triggering event, the session that produced it, the tests that validated it, and the approver who merged it. Design the traceability architecture.
Option1: Trigger layer logs event ID → Devin session URL is embedded in PR description → CI results are linked as status checks on the PR → merge requires human approval (recorded in Git history) → audit trail spans: event log → session log → PR → CI → merge commit author — all linked through the event/session/PR ID chain
Option2: Add a compliance database that records every Devin action independently from the PR system
Option3: Require Devin to write a CHANGELOG entry for every change with full event context
Option4: Configure the trigger layer to CC the compliance team email on every event

QuestionText: Design a blue-green testing pattern where Devin validates a migration by running both old and new implementations and comparing outputs before the client commits to the new system.
Option1: Devin session runs the original system with test inputs → captures outputs as golden files → runs the migrated system with the same inputs → programmatically compares outputs → PR includes the migration code + comparison report + any discrepancies highlighted → human reviews parity evidence before merging
Option2: Devin migrates the code, runs tests on the new version only, and opens a PR — the team trusts passing tests as equivalence proof
Option3: Deploy both old and new systems to staging in parallel → Devin monitors production traffic split between them for a week
Option4: Devin translates the code and the team manually compares outputs in a spreadsheet

QuestionText: A client wants to build a self-healing CI pipeline: when tests become flaky (pass/fail intermittently), Devin should automatically identify and fix the root cause. Design the detection-to-remediation architecture.
Option1: CI system tracks test result history → when a test flips pass/fail N times in M runs, a 'flaky test' event fires → trigger layer invokes Devin with test name + recent failure logs + relevant source files → Devin analyzes timing dependencies, race conditions, or environmental assumptions → opens PR with fix + idempotency key = test name to prevent duplicate sessions for the same flaky test
Option2: Configure Devin to re-run failed tests 3 times — if they pass on retry, mark them as flaky and skip them
Option3: Scheduled weekly session that deletes all tests that failed more than once in the past week
Option4: Devin monitors CI dashboards via browser automation and identifies visual patterns in test results

QuestionText: An architect proposes integrating Devin at every SDLC gate: requirement analysis, design review, implementation, testing, deployment, and monitoring. A senior engineer argues this creates over-dependency on a single platform. Evaluate the senior engineer's concern.
Option1: The concern is valid — the architecture should use the toolchain-agnostic stub pattern so each gate's automation can function independently of Devin if needed, and human processes remain viable fallbacks. Over-integration creates a single point of failure
Option2: The concern is invalid — Devin is designed to be embedded at every SDLC phase and its reliability makes single-point-of-failure concerns obsolete
Option3: The concern is only valid for production deployment — all other phases can safely depend entirely on Devin
Option4: The concern is valid but the mitigation is to run two Devin organizations in active-passive failover

QuestionText: Evaluate the following SDLC integration proposal: 'All developer PRs should be blocked from merge until Devin Review approves them, replacing one of the two required human reviewers.' What is the STRONGEST argument against this?
Option1: Devin Review excels at detecting logic errors and security issues but cannot evaluate architectural fit, business context, naming conventions in context, or team-specific design philosophy — replacing a human reviewer removes a qualitative judgment layer that automated analysis cannot replicate
Option2: Devin Review is too slow and would create a bottleneck in the merge process
Option3: Regulatory requirements always mandate two human reviewers regardless of automated tooling
Option4: Devin Review would approve too many PRs, reducing the team's merge standards

QuestionText: A client implemented event-driven triggers for every possible event (CI failure, PR opened, commit pushed, branch created, issue commented). After a month, they report 'automation fatigue' — too many low-value sessions and PRs. Evaluate the root cause and prescribe a solution.
Option1: The root cause is triggering on events without qualifying their value — the solution is to add severity/relevance filters at the trigger layer (only CRITICAL SAST findings, only CI failures on protected branches, only tickets with explicit Devin labels) and implement quiet hours or throttling to batch low-priority triggers
Option2: The solution is to disable all event-driven triggers and switch to manual invocation only
Option3: The root cause is that Devin produces low-quality PRs — improving prompt quality will eliminate fatigue
Option4: The solution is to hire a dedicated person to manage the Devin automation queue full-time

QuestionText: Evaluate the maturity of this SDLC integration: Devin sessions are triggered manually by developers pasting prompts, there is no shared context configuration, and each developer writes their own prompts with different conventions. What level would you assess and what is the highest-value improvement?
Option1: This is ad-hoc maturity (lowest level). The highest-value improvement is investing in the shared context layer — Knowledge notes for coding standards, environment blueprints for consistent builds, and Playbooks for recurring tasks — because this creates compounding value across all future sessions regardless of which developer triggers them
Option2: This is moderate maturity — the improvement is to add more developers to increase session volume
Option3: This is acceptable maturity for a team just starting — no changes needed until they outgrow manual triggers
Option4: The highest-value improvement is purchasing more ACUs so sessions run faster

QuestionText: Two architects disagree on where verification should happen in the SDLC: Architect A says Devin should verify locally on its VM (fast feedback) and only use CI as a final confirmation. Architect B says Devin should skip local testing and rely entirely on CI (single source of truth). Evaluate both positions.
Option1: Architect A's position is superior — local verification provides a seconds-fast feedback loop enabling rapid iteration, while CI serves as the authoritative gate. This matches Devin's verification model: local testing first, CI monitoring after push, iterate if CI fails. Architect B's approach wastes 45+ minutes per iteration cycle
Option2: Architect B's position is superior — CI is the single source of truth and local tests may diverge from the CI environment, creating false confidence
Option3: Both positions are equally valid depending on the CI pipeline duration
Option4: Neither is correct — Devin should verify against a staging environment instead of local or CI

QuestionText: A client wants to measure the ROI of their SDLC automation investment. They propose measuring 'number of Devin sessions per week' as the primary KPI. Evaluate this metric.
Option1: Session count is a poor primary KPI because it measures activity, not value. Better metrics include: PRs merged (output), MTTR reduction (speed), findings remediated (security posture), engineering hours freed (capacity), and first-pass success rate (quality). Volume without quality can indicate wasted ACUs on low-value triggers
Option2: Session count is a reasonable primary KPI because higher volume directly correlates with higher value delivered
Option3: Session count should be combined with ACU spend for a complete ROI picture
Option4: The best KPI is customer satisfaction score since engineering metrics are too technical for leadership

QuestionText: An architect who designs all SDLC automations to fire-and-forget (trigger Devin and never check the outcome) has a fundamentally sound architecture because Devin's verification model handles quality internally.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Evaluate the following claim: 'Event-driven Devin integration completely eliminates the need for scheduled sessions because every action can be triggered by an event.' Is this accurate?
Option1: This is incorrect — scheduled sessions serve a different purpose: proactive maintenance tasks (dependency freshness, dead code cleanup, coverage monitoring) have no external event to trigger them. They are time-driven, not event-driven. Both patterns are complementary, not substitutes
Option2: This is correct — any scheduled task can be reframed as triggered by a 'time elapsed' event, making schedules unnecessary
Option3: This is mostly correct but scheduled sessions are still needed for cost optimization (batching during off-peak hours)
Option4: This is correct for mature organizations but startups still need scheduled sessions due to fewer event sources

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: Active → Waiting for feedback → Hibernated → Resumed
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Each Devin session runs on its own isolated virtual machine — a separate VM per session, not a shared server.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Which components make up Devin's shared context layer that persists across sessions?
Option1: Environment configurations (VM blueprints)
Option2: Knowledge notes
Option3: Playbooks
Option4: MCP servers

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: A full Linux VM with shell, browser, desktop, and the ability to install packages, run builds, and connect to remote systems
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Devin's VM boots from a pre-configured snapshot defined by the organization's environment blueprint, meaning sessions start with dependencies already installed.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: ACU-based (Agent Compute Units) — you pay for active compute time only; hibernated sessions do not consume ACUs
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: Local testing on VM → Push to branch → CI monitoring and iteration → PR as review gate → External validation (optional)
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: How does Devin handle secrets and credentials?
Option1: Secrets are scoped credentials injected at session start via the platform's secrets management layer — never embedded in prompts or code, never persisted in session state
Option2: Secrets are stored in a .env file committed to the repository
Option3: Secrets are typed directly into the session prompt by the user
Option4: Secrets are downloaded from a vault at runtime via an API key stored in the VM image

QuestionText: Why does each Devin session use an isolated VM rather than sharing infrastructure with other sessions?
Option1: Isolation eliminates lateral movement risk between sessions, ensures reproducibility from a clean base, and preserves the organization's security posture — each session is scoped to exactly the resources provisioned for it
Option2: Shared infrastructure would be too expensive for the number of sessions running simultaneously
Option3: VMs provide faster execution than containers for compilation tasks
Option4: Regulatory requirements mandate VM-level isolation for all AI systems

QuestionText: Explain why Devin's hibernation model is beneficial for long-running tasks like multi-day code reviews.
Option1: Hibernation snapshots VM state and releases compute while waiting for human feedback — no cost accrues during idle time, full context is preserved, and the session resumes instantly when new input arrives, making multi-day interactions natural and cost-efficient
Option2: Hibernation speeds up the VM by freeing memory from inactive processes
Option3: Hibernation creates a backup of the session in case of VM failure
Option4: Hibernation is required because VMs have a maximum 8-hour runtime limit

QuestionText: How does the 'clean-room + shared context' architecture achieve both security and productivity simultaneously?
Option1: Each session's runtime VM is fully isolated (clean-room for security), but the organization's accumulated configuration — blueprints, Knowledge, Playbooks, MCP servers, Secrets, Git connections — flows into every session automatically (shared context for productivity)
Option2: Security comes from encrypting all data at rest, and productivity comes from using faster hardware
Option3: Clean-room means no internet access, and shared context means sessions can read each other's files
Option4: Security is handled by the organization's firewall, and productivity comes from caching build artifacts between sessions

QuestionText: What does it mean that Devin's sessions are 'reproducible from scratch'?
Option1: Every session starts from the same base snapshot defined by the environment blueprint — no accumulated state drift between sessions, no 'works on my machine' problems, and the environment configuration is codified and versioned
Option2: Devin can recreate any file it deletes by regenerating it from memory
Option3: Sessions are recorded and can be replayed step by step for auditing
Option4: Any session failure can be retried with identical inputs to produce identical outputs

QuestionText: When Devin hibernates and then resumes from a PR comment, it must re-read the entire codebase from scratch because VM memory is cleared during hibernation.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Why is the environment blueprint described as a 'one-time investment that pays dividends across every subsequent session'?
Option1: Because the blueprint defines persistent VM state (dependencies, runtimes, tools, startup scripts) that is baked into a snapshot — every future session boots with everything pre-installed, eliminating per-session setup friction and ensuring consistency
Option2: Because blueprints are shared between organizations, so one company's investment benefits all customers
Option3: Because blueprints are free to create and only cost ACUs when sessions actually run
Option4: Because a single blueprint works across all programming languages without modification

QuestionText: Explain the difference between the 'initialize' and 'maintenance' blocks in a Devin environment blueprint.
Option1: Initialize runs once during snapshot creation for persistent software installation (baked into the image); maintenance runs at every session start for ephemeral state setup (credentials, tokens, dynamic config that cannot be persisted)
Option2: Initialize sets up the IDE and maintenance keeps it updated
Option3: Initialize runs before code is cloned and maintenance runs after
Option4: Initialize is for production environments and maintenance is for development

QuestionText: How does Devin's context retrieval differ from a simple script that reads files?
Option1: Devin programmatically retrieves context from multiple sources (Git repos, DeepWiki, MCP servers, Knowledge notes, shell, browser) and builds understanding of the system before making changes — choosing what to read based on the task, not following a fixed file list
Option2: There is no difference — Devin reads files sequentially just like a script would
Option3: Devin uses a pre-built embedding index while scripts use grep
Option4: Devin can only access files explicitly listed in the prompt

QuestionText: A client's application requires Redis, PostgreSQL, and Elasticsearch for local testing. The architect needs to configure Devin's environment so every session can run the test suite. What is the correct approach?
Option1: Add Docker Compose configuration with Redis, PostgreSQL, and Elasticsearch services to the repository, then configure the environment blueprint's initialize block to install Docker and the maintenance block to start the compose stack at session start
Option2: Install Redis, PostgreSQL, and Elasticsearch natively on the VM in the initialize block and let them persist across sessions
Option3: Provide credentials to the client's shared development database cluster in Devin's secrets
Option4: Have each session install all three services from scratch in the maintenance block every time

QuestionText: A session needs to connect to the client's private Jira instance for ticket context. The Jira instance is only accessible via their corporate VPN. How should the architect configure platform access?
Option1: Configure an MCP server integration for Jira that routes through the appropriate network path (VPN, PrivateLink, or ZPA), with credentials stored as org-level secrets — this persists across all sessions without per-session VPN setup
Option2: Add VPN connection commands to every session's prompt so Devin connects each time
Option3: Mirror the Jira data into a public-facing API that Devin can access directly
Option4: Use Devin's browser to log into Jira through a web-based VPN portal each session

QuestionText: A client reports that Devin sessions take 10 minutes to install dependencies before starting actual work. The repository has a large node_modules footprint (2GB). How should the architect optimize session startup?
Option1: Move npm install into the blueprint's initialize block so dependencies are baked into the VM snapshot — sessions boot with node_modules already present, eliminating the 10-minute install on every session start
Option2: Configure Devin to use a faster internet connection for npm downloads
Option3: Switch from npm to yarn because it installs faster
Option4: Add the node_modules directory to the Git repository so it is cloned with the code

QuestionText: A client has a custom CLI tool required for their build process that is distributed as an internal binary. It requires an auth token to download. How should the architect make this available to Devin?
Option1: Store the auth token as an org-scoped secret, then in the blueprint's initialize block download and install the CLI using the secret reference ($TOKEN) — the binary persists in the snapshot, the token is used only during snapshot build
Option2: Have the user paste the download URL with embedded token in every session prompt
Option3: Ask Devin to reverse-engineer the CLI from the build output
Option4: Upload the binary as a file attachment in each session

QuestionText: A client's application requires GPU access for ML model testing. Devin's standard VMs do not have GPUs. The architect needs to design a verification strategy. What approach respects the platform's execution model?
Option1: Design the verification to delegate GPU-dependent tests to an external runner (CI pipeline with GPU runners, or a remote test endpoint) while keeping unit tests and non-GPU code changes verifiable locally on Devin's VM — Devin pushes and monitors the external CI for GPU test results
Option2: Request a GPU-enabled VM from the Devin platform for all sessions
Option3: Skip all testing and rely entirely on post-merge CI for validation
Option4: Mock the GPU dependencies in the local test environment so Devin thinks tests pass

QuestionText: A team needs Devin to maintain an application that requires license-based commercial software (e.g., Oracle database) for its test suite. Licenses are per-machine and expensive. How should the architect configure the platform?
Option1: Use Testcontainers or an in-memory database substitute for local testing on Devin's VM (avoiding per-session license costs), and rely on CI with a shared licensed environment for full integration testing — Devin validates locally with substitutes, then monitors the licensed CI pipeline
Option2: Install the licensed software in the blueprint and purchase a dedicated license for Devin's snapshot
Option3: Have Devin connect to the team's shared development Oracle instance via network for all test runs
Option4: Skip tests that require Oracle and only run unit tests that use mocks

QuestionText: A client wants to understand how Devin manages state between an initial implementation session and subsequent feedback iterations. A reviewer leaves a comment 3 days after the PR is opened. How does the platform handle this?
Option1: The session hibernated after opening the PR (preserving full VM state). When the comment arrives 3 days later, the platform resumes the VM from its snapshot — Devin has full context of what it did, reads the new comment, and iterates. No state is lost despite the 3-day gap
Option2: A new session is created that reads the PR diff and comment history to reconstruct context
Option3: The original session remains active and consuming ACUs for the full 3 days until the comment arrives
Option4: The PR is assigned to a different Devin session that specializes in handling review feedback

QuestionText: A client asks why Devin cannot simply reuse the previous session's VM for the next task instead of booting from the snapshot each time. Analyze the trade-off.
Option1: Booting fresh from the snapshot ensures reproducibility (no accumulated state drift), isolation (previous session's artifacts do not leak), and security (no credentials or data persist from one task to the next). Reusing VMs would introduce 'works on my machine' drift over time
Option2: Reusing VMs would be faster and cheaper — the fresh-boot requirement is purely a billing decision
Option3: There is no trade-off — Devin already reuses VMs for the same user's sessions within a 24-hour window
Option4: Fresh boots are only required for security-sensitive tasks; routine coding tasks can safely reuse VMs

QuestionText: Analyze the relationship between Devin's 'ephemeral testing environments' capability and the clean-room execution model. Why are they architecturally complementary?
Option1: The clean-room model guarantees the session starts from a known state; ephemeral testing environments (containers, sandboxes) deployed within that session add another isolation layer for integration testing. Both layers reinforce the principle of no persistent state leaking — the session is isolated from other sessions, and test environments are isolated from the session itself
Option2: They are the same thing — 'ephemeral testing environments' is just another name for the clean-room VM
Option3: Ephemeral testing environments are only used when the clean-room model fails
Option4: They are complementary only because both use containers under the hood

QuestionText: A client observes that Devin's local testing passed but CI failed on the same code. Analyze the possible causes within the platform architecture.
Option1: The VM's environment (snapshot-based) may differ from the CI runner's environment — different OS versions, dependency versions, or test data. The blueprint should mirror CI's environment as closely as possible, and the maintenance block should sync any dynamic test fixtures
Option2: This is impossible — if local tests pass on Devin's VM, CI must also pass since they run the same code
Option3: Devin's VM always has newer package versions than CI, so the fix is to downgrade Devin's packages
Option4: This happens because Devin runs tests in parallel while CI runs them sequentially

QuestionText: Compare the architectural implications of org-scoped secrets vs. repo-scoped secrets vs. user-scoped secrets. When is each appropriate?
Option1: Org-scoped: shared service accounts used by all sessions (e.g., shared Jira token). Repo-scoped: credentials specific to one repository's infrastructure (e.g., a repo-specific deploy key). User-scoped: personal credentials that should not be shared with other team members (e.g., personal GitHub PAT). The hierarchy prevents over-sharing while enabling appropriate access
Option2: All secrets should be org-scoped for simplicity — team members all work on the same systems
Option3: Repo-scoped secrets are always preferable because they follow the principle of least privilege
Option4: User-scoped secrets should never be used because Devin is a team resource, not personal

QuestionText: A team is debating whether to put database migration scripts in the blueprint's initialize block or maintenance block. Analyze which is correct and why.
Option1: Neither — database migrations change with code and should run as part of the session's task execution (not blueprint). The blueprint should install the migration tool (initialize) and start the database service (maintenance), but running migrations belongs to the session logic since the schema evolves with each PR
Option2: Initialize block — migrations define the persistent schema needed by all sessions
Option3: Maintenance block — migrations need to run at every session start to ensure the schema is current
Option4: Both — initialize for the baseline schema, maintenance for recent migrations

QuestionText: Analyze why Devin's architecture uses the PR as the 'review gate' rather than having an approval step within the session itself.
Option1: The PR integrates with the team's existing review tools, CI infrastructure, and approval workflows — it is a familiar, auditable artifact that provides diff visibility, threaded discussion, status checks, and merge protection. Building an in-session approval flow would duplicate what PRs already provide and fragment the audit trail
Option2: In-session approval is not possible because Devin cannot pause mid-execution
Option3: PRs are used because Devin only supports Git-based workflows and cannot produce other artifact types
Option4: The choice is arbitrary — an in-session approval step would work equally well

QuestionText: A client wants to understand the cost implications of Devin's architecture for a task that involves: 30 minutes of active coding, 2 days waiting for human review, then 15 minutes of iteration. Analyze the ACU consumption.
Option1: ACU consumption is approximately 45 minutes of active compute (30 min + 15 min). The 2 days of waiting cost zero ACUs because the session hibernates — compute resources are released while state is preserved. This makes long-lifecycle tasks (multi-day reviews, blocked dependencies) economically viable
Option2: ACU consumption is 48+ hours because the VM must remain allocated while waiting for the review
Option3: ACU consumption is 30 minutes — the iteration after review is free as a 'follow-up' within the same session
Option4: ACU consumption depends on the reviewer's response time — faster reviews use fewer ACUs

QuestionText: A client asks: 'If sessions start from a snapshot, how does Devin get the latest code? The snapshot was built days ago.' Analyze how the architecture handles this.
Option1: The snapshot bakes in tooling, runtimes, and dependencies (slow-changing). At session start, Devin clones or pulls the latest code from the Git connection (fast operation). This separation means snapshots rarely need rebuilding (only when tools change) while code is always current
Option2: Snapshots are rebuilt every time any repository has new commits
Option3: The snapshot includes a cached copy of the code that Devin patches with recent commits using git fetch
Option4: Sessions cannot access code newer than the snapshot — this is a known platform limitation

QuestionText: Analyze why Devin's platform provides both MCP server integrations AND shell/browser access. Why are both needed?
Option1: MCP provides structured, pre-configured access to common tools (Jira, Datadog, Confluence) with consistent interfaces. Shell/browser provides unrestricted access for anything not covered by MCP — custom APIs, legacy systems, internal tools. Together they cover both standardized and bespoke integration needs
Option2: MCP is for reading data and shell is for writing data — they serve complementary I/O directions
Option3: MCP is used during the initialize phase and shell/browser during active execution
Option4: Shell access is a legacy feature being replaced by MCP; new deployments should use MCP exclusively

QuestionText: A client is concerned that if a Devin session is compromised, it could access other sessions' data. Analyze the architectural safeguards against this.
Option1: Each session runs on its own isolated VM — there is no shared filesystem, no shared memory, no shared network namespace between sessions. Credentials are scoped (not global), and there is no lateral movement path between VMs. Even if one session were compromised, it has no mechanism to discover or access other sessions
Option2: Sessions share a common disk but use file permission isolation (similar to multi-tenant Linux)
Option3: All session-to-session communication is encrypted, which prevents interception but does not prevent access
Option4: The platform relies on network firewalls between session VMs but they share a common credential store

QuestionText: Design an environment blueprint for a Java/Spring Boot monorepo that requires: JDK 17, Gradle 8.x, Docker Compose (Postgres + Redis + Kafka), and connection to a private Artifactory for internal dependencies. Consider which blocks each item belongs in.
Option1: Initialize: install JDK 17, Gradle 8.x, Docker, and configure Artifactory credentials ($ARTIFACTORY_TOKEN) for Gradle's repository resolution. Maintenance: start Docker Compose stack (Postgres/Redis/Kafka), verify services are healthy, run gradle dependencies to warm the cache. This puts persistent installs in the snapshot and ephemeral services in per-session startup
Option2: Initialize: install everything including starting Docker Compose. Maintenance: just pull latest code
Option3: Initialize: only JDK and Gradle. Maintenance: install Docker, start Compose, configure Artifactory, warm cache
Option4: Put everything in maintenance so the snapshot stays small and generic

QuestionText: Design a verification strategy for a client whose application has: fast unit tests (2 min), slow integration tests (30 min), and an external contract test suite (requires staging). Map each to the appropriate verification tier in Devin's model.
Option1: Local VM: run unit tests (2 min) — fast feedback loop before pushing. CI pipeline: run integration tests (30 min) — Devin pushes, monitors CI, iterates if failures occur. External validation: contract tests against staging triggered by CI after integration tests pass — Devin monitors this final gate. This tiered approach provides increasingly rigorous verification while keeping the inner loop fast
Option2: Run all tests locally on the VM to avoid CI dependency — even if it takes 30+ minutes per iteration
Option3: Skip local testing entirely and push immediately — let CI handle all verification in parallel
Option4: Only run unit tests locally and in CI — contract tests are out of scope for Devin

QuestionText: A client has 5 product teams, each with different tech stacks (Java, Python, .NET, Go, React). Design the environment configuration architecture that balances standardization with per-team customization.
Option1: Org-level blueprint: install shared tools (Docker, Git, common CLI utilities, monitoring agents). Repo-level blueprints per tech stack: Java repos get JDK/Gradle, Python repos get pyenv/poetry, .NET repos get SDK, Go repos get Go toolchain, React repos get Node/npm. Shared Knowledge notes for org-wide conventions; team-specific Knowledge with appropriate scope triggers for each tech stack
Option2: One large org-level blueprint that installs all 5 tech stacks in every session regardless of which team triggers it
Option3: Five separate Devin organizations, one per team, with no shared configuration
Option4: No blueprints — let each session install whatever it needs from scratch since teams change stacks frequently

QuestionText: Design a platform architecture that supports both real-time emergency response (incident triage within minutes) and batch maintenance (weekly dependency updates) without one blocking the other.
Option1: Event-driven incident sessions have no concurrency limit with other session types (each gets its own VM instantly). Scheduled maintenance sessions use the parent-child pattern with controlled concurrency (batches of N). Both share the same shared context layer but execute independently — emergency sessions are never queued behind batch work because each session gets its own isolated VM
Option2: Dedicate a pool of VMs to incident response and a separate pool to batch work, with manual allocation
Option3: Priority queue system where incidents pre-empt running batch sessions and take over their VMs
Option4: Run emergency sessions on a different Devin organization with its own billing to ensure availability

QuestionText: Design a Knowledge note strategy that handles: (1) organization-wide coding standards, (2) team-specific conventions, (3) repo-specific architectural decisions, and (4) temporary project guidelines that expire.
Option1: (1) Org-wide Knowledge with broad trigger scope (e.g., 'When working on any repo'). (2) Team Knowledge with team/repo-group triggers (e.g., 'When working on backend-* repos'). (3) Repo Knowledge with repo-specific triggers. (4) Time-bounded Knowledge with triggers that reference the project name — reviewed and deleted after the project completes. Scope triggers ensure Devin retrieves only relevant notes per session
Option2: Put everything in a single comprehensive Knowledge note — Devin will ignore irrelevant parts automatically
Option3: Use only repo-level Knowledge and duplicate org-wide standards into every repo's notes
Option4: Store all guidelines in AGENTS.md files in each repo instead of using the Knowledge system

QuestionText: Design the platform configuration for a client that needs Devin to operate across 3 cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP) depending on which team's repo is being modified. Each cloud requires different credentials and network paths.
Option1: Repo-scoped secrets: AWS credentials for AWS-team repos, Azure credentials for Azure-team repos, GCP credentials for GCP-team repos. Repo-level blueprints install the appropriate CLI (aws-cli, az, gcloud) per repo. MCP servers configured per cloud for observability access. The platform's secret scoping ensures a session working on an AWS repo never has access to Azure credentials
Option2: Org-level secrets with all three cloud credentials available to every session — Devin picks the right one
Option3: Three separate Devin organizations, one per cloud provider, with users split across them
Option4: A single mega-blueprint that installs all three CLIs and configures all three clouds in every session

QuestionText: Evaluate the following claim: 'Devin's clean-room architecture is overkill for development tasks — a shared multi-tenant environment would be more cost-effective and equally safe for non-production code.'
Option1: The claim underestimates the security risk — sessions handle secrets, access private repos, and connect to internal systems. A shared environment would create lateral movement paths between sessions from different users/teams/repos. The cost of isolation (per-session VM) is marginal compared to the security, reproducibility, and auditability guarantees it provides
Option2: The claim is valid — development environments do not need production-level isolation since no customer data is involved
Option3: The claim is valid for small teams (under 10 engineers) but invalid for larger organizations
Option4: The claim would be valid if Devin only generated code suggestions without executing anything

QuestionText: A client argues: 'We should configure our blueprint with all possible tools and dependencies to handle any task, even if it makes the snapshot 50GB.' Evaluate this approach.
Option1: This is suboptimal — bloated snapshots slow boot times, increase storage costs, and violate the principle of scoping environments to what's needed. Better: org-level blueprint for universal tools, repo-level blueprints for stack-specific dependencies. Each session boots only what is relevant to its task. If the team has diverse needs, multiple focused blueprints outperform one monolithic one
Option2: This is the correct approach — larger snapshots ensure no session ever waits for installation
Option3: This is acceptable as long as the team only has one tech stack
Option4: Snapshot size does not matter because Devin always starts from a network-mounted disk

QuestionText: Evaluate the statement: 'The ACU billing model incentivizes writing bad code that requires more iterations, since Devin keeps running longer to fix its own mistakes.'
Option1: This misunderstands the incentive structure — investing in the shared context layer (better blueprints, Knowledge, Playbooks) reduces iterations and ACU spend. The architecture is designed so upfront configuration investment reduces per-session cost over time. Organizations that configure well spend fewer ACUs per merged PR
Option2: This is a valid concern — organizations should cap ACUs per session to prevent runaway spending
Option3: This is correct — the platform should switch to per-PR pricing to align incentives
Option4: ACU cost is irrelevant because it is always cheaper than an engineer's hourly rate

QuestionText: An architect who puts all environment setup commands in the maintenance block (instead of splitting between initialize and maintenance) creates a correct architecture because everything still runs at session start.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Evaluate Devin's 'service account friendly' credential model vs. the alternative of using individual developer OAuth tokens. Which is architecturally superior for a team deployment?
Option1: Service accounts are superior because they decouple agent access from individual identities — no single developer's offboarding revokes team-wide agent access, permissions are scoped to what the agent needs (not what a developer has), and audit trails clearly separate human from agent actions
Option2: Individual OAuth tokens are superior because they provide per-user attribution for every change
Option3: Both are equivalent — the choice depends on the organization's identity provider
Option4: Service accounts are only appropriate for production environments, not development agents

QuestionText: A client states: 'We do not need to invest in environment blueprints because Devin can install dependencies on the fly at session start.' Evaluate this position against the platform's design philosophy.
Option1: This defeats the purpose of the blueprint system — every session wastes 5-15 minutes on installation, costs more ACUs, risks version drift between sessions (npm install today differs from tomorrow), and misses the reproducibility guarantee. The blueprint is a one-time investment that eliminates repeated setup cost and ensures every session starts from an identical, proven base
Option2: This is a valid approach for teams with limited DevOps capacity to maintain blueprints
Option3: This is acceptable for exploratory sessions but not for production-grade automation
Option4: This only matters if sessions run frequently — teams with fewer than 10 sessions/week should skip blueprints

QuestionText: In the parent-child orchestration pattern, what are the responsibilities of the parent agent?
Option1: Analyzing scope and identifying targets
Option2: Creating a playbook encoding the methodology
Option3: Spawning child agents for each target
Option4: Monitoring progress and handling failures
Option5: Executing the implementation work for each target

QuestionText: In the parent-child pattern, each child agent runs on its own isolated VM with its own branch and produces its own PR independently of other children.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: What is the purpose of a Playbook in multi-agent campaigns?
Option1: To encode a proven methodology that every child agent follows, ensuring consistent execution across all targets regardless of which team member triggered the campaign
Option2: To store the source code that child agents will modify
Option3: To schedule when each child agent starts and stops
Option4: To monitor the CI pipeline results for all children simultaneously

QuestionText: What characteristic of Playbooks makes them suitable for scaled campaigns?
Option1: They are repeatable, versionable, shareable, and composable — encoding methodology rather than target-specific details so the same playbook works across many different targets
Option2: They can only be used with parent-child patterns, not single-agent tasks
Option3: They automatically detect which repositories need changes
Option4: They replace the need for human code review on child agent PRs

QuestionText: Why does the parent-child pattern specify that 'failures in one child do not affect others'?
Option1: Because each child runs on its own isolated VM with its own branch — a compilation error, test failure, or crash in Child A's environment has no impact on Child B's environment or execution. They share no state
Option2: Because the parent agent automatically fixes child failures before they propagate
Option3: Because children are spawned in sequence, so a failure stops only subsequent children
Option4: Because children share a VM but use separate containers within it

QuestionText: Why should a Playbook 'encode methodology, not specifics'?
Option1: Because a playbook used across N targets must be generic enough to apply to each one — it describes the procedure (steps, verification criteria, rollback approach) while the session prompt or context provides target-specific details (repo name, module, version). This separation enables reuse at scale
Option2: Because specific details change too frequently to maintain in a playbook
Option3: Because specifics would make the playbook file too large for the platform to store
Option4: Because only parent agents use playbooks and parents never handle specifics

QuestionText: Why is maximum concurrency control important when spawning child agents in a campaign?
Option1: To avoid overwhelming CI systems with too many simultaneous PRs, respecting API rate limits, and preventing the human review queue from being flooded with more PRs than the team can process in a reasonable timeframe
Option2: Because the Devin platform has a hard limit of 5 concurrent sessions per organization
Option3: Because child agents on the same repo would create merge conflicts if they all run simultaneously
Option4: To reduce ACU cost since more concurrent sessions are billed at a higher rate

QuestionText: A well-designed campaign playbook should include target-specific details like file paths and variable names for each individual migration unit.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: A client has 50 microservices that need upgrading from Spring Boot 2.7 to 3.2. Each service is in its own repository. Design the campaign structure.
Option1: Parent agent: enumerate all 50 repos, create a Spring Boot migration playbook (Jakarta namespace, dependency updates, test verification), spawn 50 child agents (one per repo) each following the playbook, set concurrency limit to 10 to manage review load, monitor results and report failures to human team
Option2: Single parent session that sequentially checks out each repo, applies the upgrade, and opens 50 PRs from one session
Option3: 50 separate scheduled sessions with the same prompt — no parent coordination needed
Option4: One child agent per service but no parent — let children self-organize through a shared database

QuestionText: An organization has 500 open Snyk findings across 80 repositories. The security team wants to remediate all HIGH findings within a week. How should the architect structure this campaign?
Option1: Parent agent: query Snyk API for all HIGH findings grouped by repo → create remediation playbook (read advisory, apply fix, verify scan clean) → spawn children batched by repo (one child per repo with its findings) → set concurrency appropriate to the team's review capacity → parent tracks remediation progress and escalates stubborn findings
Option2: One child per finding (500 children) each opening its own PR — maximum parallelism
Option3: A single long-running session that iterates through all 500 findings sequentially
Option4: Assign 80 scheduled sessions (one per repo) that each handle all findings in their repo

QuestionText: A client wants to generate unit tests for 200 modules with low coverage. Some modules have dependencies on other modules. How should the architect handle the dependency consideration?
Option1: Child agents should operate independently since each writes tests for its own module using mocks/stubs for dependencies — inter-module dependencies do not create cross-child dependencies because tests isolate the unit under test. The playbook should specify to follow existing test patterns and mock external dependencies
Option2: Spawn children in dependency order — test leaf modules first, then modules that depend on them
Option3: Group dependent modules together so one child handles all modules in a dependency chain
Option4: Have the parent agent resolve all dependency conflicts before spawning any children

QuestionText: A campaign has 30 children but the team can only review 10 PRs per day. How should the architect configure concurrency?
Option1: Set concurrency to 10 children at a time — once the first batch's PRs are reviewed and merged, spawn the next batch. This prevents the review queue from growing unbounded while still parallelizing within each batch
Option2: Spawn all 30 simultaneously — the review bottleneck is the team's problem, not the architect's
Option3: Spawn 1 child at a time to match the slowest reviewer's pace
Option4: Spawn all 30 but configure Devin to auto-merge if CI passes, eliminating the review bottleneck

QuestionText: A data migration campaign involves translating 300 SAS ETL jobs to Python. Each job takes 15-45 minutes. The client wants the campaign completed within 2 weeks. Design the batching strategy.
Option1: Batch into groups of 30 children, run 10 batches sequentially. First batch validates the playbook approach — if systematic issues emerge, fix the playbook before proceeding. This allows 300 jobs across 10 days with review capacity alignment. Include golden-file validation in the playbook so each child verifies output parity before submitting its PR
Option2: Spawn all 300 simultaneously on day 1 to maximize parallelism and finish in hours
Option3: Run 5 children per day (300/60 = too slow) to be conservative
Option4: Translate all 300 in a single parent session to maintain consistency across translations

QuestionText: A campaign requires sequential dependencies: Step 1 (migrate database schema) must complete before Step 2 (update application code) which must complete before Step 3 (update integration tests). These are for the SAME repository. How should this be structured?
Option1: This is NOT a parallel campaign — it is a single-agent sequential task because all steps target the same repo and branch with ordering dependencies. Use one Devin session with a multi-step playbook that executes steps in order, verifying each before proceeding to the next
Option2: Spawn 3 child agents (one per step) running simultaneously on separate branches, then merge in order
Option3: Parent agent spawns Step 1 child, waits for merge, spawns Step 2 child, waits for merge, spawns Step 3 child
Option4: Use a chain pattern: Child 1 triggers Child 2 via webhook when its PR merges, which triggers Child 3

QuestionText: A campaign of 40 children has completed with 35 successes and 5 failures. The failures all report the same error: 'cannot resolve internal dependency X.' Analyze the root cause in the context of campaign architecture.
Option1: The playbook or environment configuration is missing access to an internal artifact repository where dependency X is published — this is a systematic issue affecting all repos that use X. The fix is to add the repository credentials/configuration to the blueprint or secrets, not to retry the failed children individually
Option2: Five repositories have a unique bug that prevents compilation — each needs individual investigation
Option3: The concurrency was too high and the internal repository rate-limited the requests
Option4: The parent agent incorrectly assigned these 5 targets to children — they should have been excluded from the campaign

QuestionText: An architect proposed spawning 200 children simultaneously for a migration campaign. After launch, the organization's GitHub instance became unresponsive due to API rate limiting. Analyze the architectural failure.
Option1: The architect failed to set a maximum concurrency limit — 200 simultaneous sessions all cloning repos, pushing branches, and opening PRs overwhelmed GitHub's API rate limits. The correct design uses batched concurrency (e.g., groups of 20) to stay within API rate limits while still parallelizing work
Option2: GitHub was experiencing an unrelated outage that coincided with the campaign launch
Option3: The parent agent should have used a different version control system for large campaigns
Option4: 200 children is within normal parameters — the issue is that GitHub needs a higher rate limit tier

QuestionText: Compare two campaign strategies for 100 security findings across 20 repos: (A) One child per finding (100 children, potentially multiple PRs per repo), (B) One child per repo (20 children, each handling all findings in that repo). Analyze which is superior and why.
Option1: Strategy B (per-repo) is superior — it avoids merge conflicts from multiple PRs targeting the same repo, reduces review overhead (20 PRs vs. 100), allows the child to see interactions between fixes in the same codebase, and uses fewer total sessions. Strategy A risks children creating conflicting changes in the same files
Option2: Strategy A (per-finding) is superior — it provides better isolation and if one fix breaks something, it only affects that one finding
Option3: Both are equivalent — the total amount of work is the same regardless of grouping
Option4: Strategy A is superior for CRITICAL findings and Strategy B for HIGH findings

QuestionText: A team ran a campaign of 50 children and noticed the first 5 children all made the same mistake — they used a deprecated API that the playbook did not account for. The remaining 45 children made the same mistake. Analyze what should have been done differently.
Option1: The campaign should have used progressive batching — run the first batch of 5, validate their output before launching the remaining 45. This 'canary batch' approach catches systematic playbook issues early. After seeing the first batch's mistake, the team would fix the playbook (add guidance about the deprecated API) and then run the remaining 45 with the corrected methodology
Option2: The playbook should have listed every deprecated API in the entire ecosystem to prevent this
Option3: Each child should have been configured to ask the user before using any API call
Option4: The parent should monitor every child in real time and intervene the moment a mistake is detected

QuestionText: Analyze why the parent-child pattern is described as enabling 'work that would otherwise require a dedicated team.' What structural property of the pattern creates this capability?
Option1: Each child agent is fully independent (own VM, own branch, own PR) and follows the same playbook — this means N units of work execute in parallel without coordination overhead between them. A human team doing the same work would need coordination meetings, shared branch management, and sequential code reviews. The pattern converts team-coordination problems into independent parallel execution
Option2: Child agents work faster than human engineers because they do not take breaks
Option3: The parent agent replaces the need for a team lead who coordinates work
Option4: Multiple child agents share a VM pool which is more efficient than dedicated hardware per developer

QuestionText: A campaign playbook instructs children to 'upgrade the framework to the latest version.' This caused problems when version 5.0 was released mid-campaign, resulting in some children using 4.9 and others using 5.0. Analyze the playbook design flaw.
Option1: The playbook uses a relative target ('latest') instead of an absolute target (specific version number). Playbooks for campaigns should pin the target version explicitly so all children produce consistent results regardless of when they execute. The version should be a parameter set at campaign launch time
Option2: The playbook should check if a new version was released since the campaign started and abort if so
Option3: This is not a flaw — some children getting the newer version is actually better
Option4: The parent should pause the entire campaign whenever a new version is released

QuestionText: A client wants to use parent-child for a task where 20 microservices need coordinated API changes — Service A's contract change affects Services B, C, and D. Analyze whether parent-child is appropriate here.
Option1: Parent-child is NOT appropriate for interdependent changes — the pattern works for independent units of work. Coordinated API changes require sequencing (change the contract first, then update consumers) and cross-repo validation.
