This is better handled as a multi-step single-session task or sequential sessions with the parent managing the order and verifying integration between steps
Option2: Parent-child is appropriate — spawn one child per service and let them coordinate through their shared CI pipeline
Option3: Parent-child is appropriate if you add a 'coordination channel' where children share state
Option4: Parent-child works perfectly — just spawn all 20 simultaneously since they are all independent services

QuestionText: Analyze the role of the parent agent's 'monitors progress, handles failures' responsibility. What specific actions should a well-designed parent take when a child fails?
Option1: The parent should: (1) classify the failure type (systematic vs. idiosyncratic), (2) if systematic, pause the campaign and fix the root cause (playbook/blueprint issue), (3) if idiosyncratic, decide whether to retry the child or escalate to humans, (4) track success/failure metrics and report aggregate campaign status
Option2: The parent should simply retry the failed child with the same parameters up to 10 times
Option3: The parent should terminate the entire campaign and report all failures to the user at once
Option4: The parent should ignore failures and report only the successes to the user

QuestionText: A team asks: 'Why can't we just use 50 scheduled sessions instead of parent-child for our 50-repo upgrade?' Analyze the differences between these approaches.
Option1: Parent-child provides: (1) a single campaign view with aggregate progress tracking, (2) consistent methodology enforcement via a shared playbook, (3) adaptive behavior (parent can pause/adjust based on early results), (4) failure handling and escalation. 50 independent scheduled sessions have no coordination, no shared methodology enforcement, no ability to detect systematic issues across the group, and no aggregate status view
Option2: There is no difference — 50 scheduled sessions achieve the same result as parent-child with less complexity
Option3: The only difference is cost — parent-child uses fewer ACUs because the parent coordinates efficiently
Option4: Scheduled sessions are superior because they do not require a parent agent consuming ACUs

QuestionText: A campaign produces 50 PRs in 2 days. The engineering team's reviewers are overwhelmed and start rubber-stamping approvals without thorough review. Analyze this as a systemic risk and identify the architectural mitigation.
Option1: The campaign concurrency was miscalibrated to the team's review capacity. Mitigations: (1) set concurrency to match daily review throughput, (2) add Devin Review as a first-pass automated reviewer to reduce human review burden, (3) use progressive batching so the first batch is reviewed carefully and validates the pattern, and subsequent batches receive lighter review since the methodology is proven
Option2: This is a human process problem, not an architectural one — the solution is to hire more reviewers
Option3: Enable auto-merge for all campaign PRs where CI passes to eliminate the review bottleneck entirely
Option4: Reduce the number of targets to match the team's capacity rather than running the full campaign

QuestionText: Design a campaign architecture for a COBOL-to-Java migration with 200 copybooks. The client requires: output parity verification, progressive validation, and the ability to pause if quality drops below threshold.
Option1: Parent: analyze all 200 copybooks → create migration playbook with golden-file verification (run COBOL output, run Java output, compare) → start with canary batch of 10 → validate output parity metrics → if parity rate > 95%, proceed with batches of 20 → parent tracks per-batch parity rate → if any batch drops below threshold, pause campaign, analyze failures, refine playbook → resume with corrected methodology
Option2: Spawn all 200 simultaneously, check parity after all complete, and redo any failures
Option3: Migrate 1 copybook per day for 200 days to ensure quality through slow, careful execution
Option4: Single session that translates all 200 sequentially to maintain consistency

QuestionText: Design an orchestration architecture for a client that needs: (1) quarterly framework upgrades across 30 services, (2) monthly security remediations across the same services, and (3) weekly dependency bumps. These campaigns may overlap temporally.
Option1: Three independent campaign types with isolation: (1) Quarterly upgrade campaign — parent spawns children per service, each on a dedicated branch pattern (upgrade/service-name). (2) Monthly security — per-repo children on security/remediation branches. (3) Weekly deps — per-repo children on deps/weekly branches. Branch naming prevents conflicts between overlapping campaigns. Concurrency limits per campaign prevent total session overload. Playbooks are distinct per campaign type
Option2: A single master parent that manages all three campaign types and ensures they never overlap temporally
Option3: Lock mechanism where only one campaign can run at a time — queue others until the active one completes
Option4: Merge all three concerns into one monthly mega-campaign that does upgrades + security + deps in one pass

QuestionText: Design a playbook for a security remediation campaign that handles three severity levels differently: CRITICAL (fix immediately and notify security team), HIGH (fix and await normal review), MEDIUM (fix and batch into weekly PR).
Option1: Playbook steps: (1) Read finding details from prompt context. (2) Classify severity. (3) For CRITICAL: apply fix → run SAST re-scan → open PR with 'urgent' label → post to security channel via Slack MCP. (4) For HIGH: apply fix → run re-scan → open standard PR. (5) For MEDIUM: apply fix → run re-scan → commit to a 'weekly-remediation' branch (append, don't replace). Parent batches MEDIUMs and opens a combined PR weekly
Option2: Same playbook for all severities — just change the PR title to indicate urgency
Option3: Three completely separate playbooks with no shared logic between them
Option4: Children for CRITICAL run instantly; HIGH and MEDIUM children are queued and run later based on a timer

QuestionText: Design a campaign monitoring dashboard architecture. The parent agent needs to provide: real-time status per child, aggregate metrics (success rate, average duration), and alerting when failure rate exceeds threshold.
Option1: Parent agent: maintain a tracking structure mapping child session IDs to targets/status → poll child sessions periodically → compute aggregate metrics (completed/failed/in-progress, avg duration, success rate) → if failure rate > threshold (e.g., 20%), pause campaign and alert human via Slack/Teams MCP → provide summary report as a GitHub issue or message with per-child status table
Option2: Each child agent posts its own status to a shared spreadsheet that humans monitor
Option3: Build a custom web dashboard that connects to the Devin API and displays real-time metrics
Option4: Rely on email notifications from individual child PR creations for monitoring

QuestionText: Design a multi-phase campaign where Phase 1 generates analysis reports (no code changes), humans review reports and approve/modify approach, then Phase 2 implements changes based on approved approaches. How do the two phases connect?
Option1: Phase 1: parent spawns analysis children (one per target) → each produces a markdown report as a PR or issue → humans review and annotate with approved approach. Phase 2: parent reads approved reports/annotations → spawns implementation children with the approved approach embedded in each child's prompt → implementation follows the human-validated plan. The connection point is the human review of Phase 1 output before Phase 2 launches
Option2: Phase 1 children directly trigger Phase 2 children via webhook when their reports are merged
Option3: Phase 1 and Phase 2 run simultaneously — Phase 2 children read Phase 1 reports in real time
Option4: A single playbook handles both phases sequentially in one child session per target

QuestionText: Design a campaign failover strategy: if a child fails after 3 retries, the parent should attempt an alternative approach before escalating. For a dependency upgrade campaign, what might this look like?
Option1: Primary playbook: upgrade to target version, fix compilation errors, run tests. If child fails 3 times: parent spawns a new child with an alternative playbook — e.g., upgrade to an intermediate version first (step-wise migration), or apply only the security patch without the full upgrade. If alternative also fails: escalate to human with diagnostic report containing all failure logs from both approaches
Option2: After 3 failures, mark the target as 'impossible to upgrade' and skip it permanently
Option3: After 3 failures, spawn 5 more children with the same playbook hoping one succeeds
Option4: After 3 failures, have the parent agent attempt the upgrade itself instead of using a child

QuestionText: Design a campaign for onboarding 50 new repositories into the Devin platform. Each repo needs: environment blueprint, Knowledge notes for conventions, initial test coverage baseline, and documentation of the build process.
Option1: Parent: enumerate 50 repos → create onboarding playbook: (1) analyze repo structure and tech stack, (2) generate environment blueprint draft (initialize/maintenance blocks), (3) document build/test commands as Knowledge notes, (4) run test suite and report coverage baseline, (5) output onboarding report PR. Spawn children in batches of 10 → human reviews each onboarding report and approves/modifies blueprints → approved blueprints are committed to each repo
Option2: Single parent session that onboards all 50 repos sequentially in one long run
Option3: Manually onboard 1 repo, then use that as a template applied identically to all 49 others
Option4: Skip per-repo onboarding — create one org-level blueprint that works for all repos

QuestionText: Design a campaign success criteria framework for a migration project. The client needs to define what 'done' means for each child, the campaign overall, and how to handle partial completion.
Option1: Per-child success: (1) code compiles, (2) all existing tests pass, (3) output parity verified against golden files, (4) SAST scan clean, (5) PR opened and CI green. Campaign success: >95% of children meet all criteria. Partial completion: children that fail criteria are categorized (systematic issue vs. complex edge case) → systematic issues trigger playbook revision and retry → complex edge cases are escalated to human engineers with diagnostic context → campaign report documents completed + escalated items
Option2: Campaign is done when all 100% of children produce merged PRs — any failure means the campaign failed
Option3: Success means children opened PRs — whether the PRs are correct is determined by human review later
Option4: Define a single SLA (all children must complete within 48 hours) as the only success metric

QuestionText: Design a testing strategy for a new playbook before running it at scale. The playbook will be used for a 100-target campaign.
Option1: (1) Run the playbook on 1 representative target manually (single session, observe behavior). (2) Run on 3-5 diverse targets (different sizes, complexities) to validate generalization. (3) Review all output PRs for quality, consistency, and edge case handling. (4) Iterate on playbook based on findings. (5) Only after the small batch validates successfully, launch the full 100-target campaign with progressive batching. This 'playbook testing' phase prevents systematic errors at scale
Option2: Launch the full 100-target campaign immediately — the first few children serve as the test
Option3: Write unit tests for the playbook itself and run them in CI before deployment
Option4: Have a senior engineer read the playbook and approve it — no actual test execution needed

QuestionText: Design an orchestration pattern for a client that needs to migrate a monolith to microservices. The work involves: (1) identifying bounded contexts, (2) extracting interfaces, (3) creating new services, (4) migrating data, (5) setting up CI/CD per service. These are NOT independent.
Option1: This is a multi-phase sequential campaign, NOT a parallel one. Phase 1: single research session identifies bounded contexts → human approves decomposition plan. Phase 2: sequential extraction — one service at a time (single agent), since extracting Service A changes the monolith that Service B will be extracted from. Phase 3 (after all extractions): parallel campaign for CI/CD setup across all new services (independent). The key insight is that only truly independent work benefits from parallel children
Option2: Spawn a child per microservice to extract — they can all work in parallel on different bounded contexts
Option3: One giant session that handles the entire monolith decomposition from start to finish
Option4: Run 5 parallel extraction campaigns and merge the resulting code at the end

QuestionText: An architect proposes using a single parent session to manage a 200-service migration campaign, spawning all children simultaneously. A colleague suggests batching in groups of 20. Which evaluation of the batched approach is MOST accurate?
Option1: Batching is superior — it allows the team to validate the playbook on the first batch, catch systematic issues early, avoid overwhelming the review queue with 200 simultaneous PRs, stay within API rate limits, and adjust the approach based on early results before committing the remaining 180 targets to a potentially flawed methodology
Option2: Batching is inferior because it takes 10x longer to complete the campaign without any quality benefit
Option3: Batching and simultaneous approaches are equivalent — the total work and ACU cost is the same
Option4: Batching is only appropriate when children have dependencies between them, which migration units do not

QuestionText: Evaluate the claim: 'For a 50-target campaign, using one generic playbook is always better than using target-specific prompts for each child.'
Option1: This is generally true but oversimplified. A generic playbook ensures consistency and is maintainable, but child prompts should still include target-specific context (repo name, specific files, known quirks) alongside the shared playbook. The ideal is: shared methodology (playbook) + target-specific context (prompt) = consistent approach with local adaptation
Option2: This is always true — target-specific prompts create inconsistency and are unmaintainable at scale
Option3: This is always false — every target is unique and deserves a custom approach
Option4: Playbooks and prompts serve the same purpose — use one or the other, never both

QuestionText: Evaluate this campaign result: 45/50 children succeeded, 5 failed with diverse errors (1 network timeout, 1 test environment issue, 2 complex edge cases, 1 missing dependency). The team wants to mark the campaign as 'complete.' Is this appropriate?
Option1: Partially appropriate — the 45 successes represent campaign completion for those targets. The 5 failures should be categorized: network timeout and test environment issues are retryable (retry once after fixing infrastructure). Complex edge cases should be escalated to human engineers. Missing dependency should be fixed in the blueprint and retried. Mark campaign as '90% complete' with clear disposition for each failure category
Option2: No — a campaign is only complete when 100% of targets succeed. Retry all 5 failures
Option3: Yes — 90% success rate is excellent and the remaining 5 are not worth the effort to fix
Option4: No — the diverse errors indicate a fundamental playbook problem that requires a full campaign redo

QuestionText: Evaluate the following principle: 'A well-designed campaign should work with zero human intervention after launch.' Is this achievable and desirable?
Option1: This is neither fully achievable nor desirable. Human intervention serves critical functions: playbook validation (first batch review), edge case handling (complex failures the playbook cannot anticipate), quality assurance (spot-checking PR output), and strategic decisions (pause/continue/modify). The goal is minimal intervention (only at decision points), not zero intervention
Option2: This is both achievable and desirable — the entire point of automation is eliminating human involvement
Option3: This is achievable for dependency bumps but not for migrations — it depends on campaign type
Option4: This is desirable but not achievable given current technology limitations — AI is not reliable enough

QuestionText: A team ran a 100-child campaign and merged all PRs. Post-merge, they discovered that 15 children used a deprecated API pattern that the playbook did not prohibit. Evaluate the failure and assign responsibility.
Option1: The failure is in playbook design and campaign validation, not in the children. The playbook should have explicitly prohibited the deprecated pattern (or the Knowledge notes should have flagged it). The campaign should have used a canary batch with human review to catch this before scaling. Responsibility: the architect who designed the campaign without adequate quality gates
Option2: The failure is Devin's — an intelligent agent should know not to use deprecated APIs without being told
Option3: The failure is the reviewers' — they approved 15 PRs without catching the deprecated usage
Option4: The failure is acceptable — deprecated does not mean broken, and the code works correctly

QuestionText: In a well-designed campaign, all children should share a single branch rather than individual branches, because this reduces the total number of PRs the team must review.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: What are the six components of Devin's shared context layer?
Option1: Environment configurations (VM blueprints)
Option2: Knowledge notes
Option3: Playbooks
Option4: MCP servers

QuestionText: Knowledge notes are persistent, human-curated context (coding standards, architecture decisions, team conventions) that Devin retrieves automatically based on the task at hand.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: The following statement is correct: At the organization level — configured once and available to every session without per-session setup
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: What are Automations in the Devin platform context?
Option1: Webhooks, scheduled sessions, and trigger rules configured at the organization level — every team member's events route through the same automation layer
Option2: Scripts that run inside Devin sessions to automate repetitive coding tasks
Option3: GitHub Actions workflows that the platform generates automatically
Option4: Pre-built playbooks that execute without human approval

QuestionText: What is the relationship between a Devin organization and its sessions regarding configuration?
Option1: Configuration (blueprints, Knowledge, Playbooks, MCP, Secrets, Git connections) is set once at the org level, and every session from any team member automatically inherits it — making setup a one-time team investment
Option2: Each session has its own independent configuration that must be set up from scratch
Option3: Configuration is stored per-user and sessions inherit from the triggering user's settings
Option4: Configuration is stored in the repository's .devin/ folder and read at clone time

QuestionText: Why is Devin described as a 'team-based coworker agent' rather than an 'individual user's AI assistant'?
Option1: Because configuration, knowledge, and integrations belong to the team, not to any single user's session — the shared context layer means one engineer's setup investment benefits every subsequent session from any team member, and multiple users can collaborate on the same Devin PR
Option2: Because Devin can only be used by teams of 5 or more engineers
Option3: Because Devin requires team approval before starting any session
Option4: Because Devin's output is attributed to the team rather than individual contributors

QuestionText: Explain why Knowledge note scope/triggers are important rather than making all notes available to every session.
Option1: Scope/triggers ensure Devin retrieves only the relevant subset of knowledge for each task — preventing context pollution from irrelevant notes, reducing noise, and keeping the effective context focused. A Java session does not need Python conventions, and a frontend task does not need database migration rules
Option2: Scoping reduces the storage cost of Knowledge notes in the platform database
Option3: Without scoping, sessions would take longer to start because they load all notes
Option4: Scoping is a security measure to prevent teams from reading each other's proprietary knowledge

QuestionText: Why does the continuous improvement cycle show 'first sessions' leading to 'playbooks are created for recurring tasks'?
Option1: Because initial sessions reveal which tasks are recurring and what methodology works best — this operational experience is then codified into playbooks so future sessions execute proven procedures consistently, rather than re-discovering the approach each time
Option2: Because playbooks can only be created after a session has been run at least once
Option3: Because the platform automatically generates playbooks from session history
Option4: Because playbooks require test data that only comes from initial session output

QuestionText: The shared context layer means that if one engineer configures a Jira MCP integration, every other engineer's Devin sessions can also access Jira without any additional setup.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Why is 'Git connections' part of the shared context layer rather than being configured per session?
Option1: Because repository access should be consistent across all sessions in an organization — configuring it once at the org level ensures every session can clone and push to connected repos immediately without per-session authentication setup, and access can be managed centrally
Option2: Because Git connections require complex SSH key exchanges that are too slow to do per session
Option3: Because only organization administrators should have the ability to connect repositories
Option4: Because Git connections consume a license seat and org-level sharing reduces cost

QuestionText: A new client is onboarding to Devin. They have 20 repositories, a Jira instance, a Datadog account, and use GitHub for version control. List the configuration steps in order of priority.
Option1: (1) Git connections (enable repo access), (2) Secrets (API keys, service account credentials), (3) Environment blueprints (toolchain per tech stack), (4) MCP servers (Jira + Datadog integration), (5) Knowledge notes (coding standards, conventions), (6) Playbooks (recurring task methodologies) — foundations first, then intelligence
Option2: (1) Playbooks for all common tasks, (2) Knowledge notes, (3) MCP servers, (4) Git connections, (5) Secrets, (6) Blueprints
Option3: (1) Purchase maximum ACU allocation, (2) Create user accounts for all engineers, (3) Configure everything else later
Option4: (1) MCP servers, (2) Knowledge notes, (3) Playbooks, (4) Blueprints, (5) Secrets, (6) Git connections

QuestionText: A client's team has strict coding standards: tabs for indentation, no default exports in TypeScript, and all API responses must use a standard wrapper type. How should the architect encode this for Devin?
Option1: Create Knowledge notes with specific scope triggers (e.g., 'When working on TypeScript repos in this org') that document each standard with examples. Additionally, configure linting rules (ESLint/Prettier) in the blueprint so standards are enforced automatically — Knowledge provides context, linting provides enforcement
Option2: Add the standards to every session's prompt as a prefix paragraph
Option3: Configure a pre-commit hook and let Devin discover the standards through trial and error when commits are rejected
Option4: Create a single Knowledge note with all standards for all languages used by the organization

QuestionText: A client wants Devin to update Jira ticket status when it opens a PR. Which configuration components are needed?
Option1: (1) Jira MCP server configured with appropriate credentials, (2) Knowledge note instructing Devin to update ticket status when opening PRs (with the transition workflow specifics), (3) Org-level secret for the Jira API token — these three enable the behavior across all sessions without per-session configuration
Option2: A custom webhook from GitHub to Jira that fires on PR creation events
Option3: A dedicated Devin session that polls for new PRs and updates corresponding Jira tickets
Option4: Add 'update Jira status' to every session prompt manually

QuestionText: A client's Python team uses poetry for dependency management, pytest for testing, and ruff for linting. Their Node.js team uses pnpm, vitest, and eslint. Design the blueprint architecture.
Option1: Org-level blueprint: install common tools (Docker, Git, shared CLI utilities). Python repo-level blueprints: install Python, poetry, configure virtualenv, install ruff. Node.js repo-level blueprints: install Node.js via nvm, pnpm, global eslint. Each repo's blueprint tailors the environment to its tech stack while sharing the org-level foundation
Option2: One org-level blueprint with both Python and Node.js toolchains installed for all sessions
Option3: No blueprints — include installation commands in every session prompt
Option4: Separate Devin organizations for the Python team and Node.js team

QuestionText: A client is designing their Playbook library. They have recurring tasks: framework upgrades, security remediations, test generation, and incident response. Design the playbook hierarchy.
Option1: Four distinct playbooks, each encoding methodology (not target specifics): (1) Framework upgrade playbook: check compatibility → update deps → fix breaking changes → run tests → update docs. (2) Security remediation: read advisory → apply fix → re-scan → verify clean. (3) Test generation: analyze module → identify patterns → write tests → verify coverage target. (4) Incident response: query logs → identify root cause → implement fix → verify → update runbook. Each is reusable across any repo/target
Option2: One master playbook with conditional branches for each task type
Option3: Separate playbooks per repository, each containing all four task types for that repo
Option4: Playbooks are not needed — Knowledge notes can serve the same purpose with less maintenance

QuestionText: A client has sensitive database credentials that only the backend team should access, and a Slack webhook token that all teams can use. How should the architect scope these secrets?
Option1: Database credentials: repo-scoped secrets attached only to backend repositories (sessions working on frontend repos never see them). Slack webhook token: org-scoped secret available to all sessions. This applies least-privilege scoping — each session only has access to credentials relevant to its task
Option2: Both as org-scoped secrets — trust that Devin will only use credentials relevant to the current task
Option3: Both as user-scoped secrets — each developer brings their own credentials
Option4: Database credentials in the backend repo's .env file, Slack token as an org secret

QuestionText: A client has a domain glossary (business terms mapped to code concepts) and architecture decision records (ADRs). Where should each live in the context layer?
Option1: Domain glossary: Knowledge note with broad scope trigger ('When working on any repo in this org') since business terminology applies across the codebase. ADRs: Knowledge notes with repo/domain-specific triggers (e.g., 'When working on the payments service') since architectural decisions apply to specific bounded contexts
Option2: Both in a single Knowledge note titled 'Organization Context' for simplicity
Option3: Domain glossary in Knowledge, ADRs in Playbooks since they describe procedures
Option4: Both in AGENTS.md files in each repository rather than using the Knowledge system

QuestionText: A client's organization has 100 engineers across 8 teams. They want to configure Devin such that each team has customized Knowledge and Playbooks while sharing org-wide standards. Design the configuration architecture.
Option1: Org-wide Knowledge notes: shared coding standards, security requirements, git conventions (broad scope triggers). Team-specific Knowledge notes: team conventions, domain terminology, architectural decisions (scoped to that team's repos). Shared Playbooks: common tasks (dep bumps, security). Team Playbooks: domain-specific procedures. All within a single org — scope triggers create logical separation without physical org boundaries
Option2: 8 separate Devin organizations, one per team, each with its own Knowledge and Playbooks
Option3: All Knowledge in one shared pool with no scoping — Devin will determine what is relevant
Option4: Team-specific configurations only, no shared org-wide standards to avoid conflicts

QuestionText: A client reports that Devin sessions are inconsistent — some follow team conventions perfectly while others ignore them. Analyze the likely configuration gap.
Option1: The coding conventions are likely not encoded in Knowledge notes (or have overly narrow scope triggers that don't match all relevant sessions). Without explicit Knowledge configuration, Devin relies on what it can infer from the code — which may be inconsistent. The fix is to create Knowledge notes with proper scope triggers that activate for all relevant repos
Option2: Devin's AI model has inherent randomness that causes inconsistent behavior
Option3: The team has too many conventions and Devin becomes confused by contradictions
Option4: The inconsistency is caused by different engineers writing different quality prompts

QuestionText: Analyze why the platform provides three configuration mechanisms (Knowledge notes, AGENTS.md, and linting rules) for encoding team standards rather than just one.
Option1: Each serves a different purpose: Knowledge notes provide contextual guidance retrieved by scope triggers (team conventions, architecture decisions). AGENTS.md is always available within its repo and is version-controlled with the code (repo-specific rules). Linting rules provide automated enforcement (hard constraints, not guidance). Together they cover guidance, per-repo rules, and enforcement — three layers that complement each other
Option2: They are redundant — the platform should consolidate into one mechanism
Option3: Knowledge notes are for general standards, AGENTS.md for security, and linting for style
Option4: They exist for backward compatibility — new users should only use Knowledge notes

QuestionText: A client has 50 Knowledge notes and reports that sessions are slow because Devin 'reads too much context.' Analyze the problem and identify the architectural solution.
Option1: The scope triggers are likely too broad — many notes are being retrieved for sessions where they are not relevant, creating noise. The solution is to refine scope triggers to be more specific (e.g., change 'When working on code' to 'When working on Python backend services') and potentially consolidate overlapping notes into fewer, well-scoped items
Option2: 50 Knowledge notes is too many — the platform has a practical limit of 20 notes per org
Option3: Knowledge notes should be shortened to single sentences to reduce reading time
Option4: Move all Knowledge into AGENTS.md files in each repo to avoid the retrieval overhead

QuestionText: Compare the maintenance burden of org-level vs. repo-level blueprints. A client asks: 'Should we put everything in the org blueprint for simplicity?'
Option1: Org-level blueprints change rarely (shared tools used by everyone) and affect ALL sessions — changes require careful validation. Repo-level blueprints change with the tech stack of individual projects and affect only that repo's sessions. Putting everything org-level creates bloated images, slower boot times, and a single-change-breaks-everyone risk. The correct architecture uses org-level for universal tools and repo-level for stack-specific dependencies
Option2: Yes, one org blueprint is simpler and the maintenance burden is lower than managing 20 repo blueprints
Option3: No, everything should be repo-level to avoid the risk of org-level changes breaking all sessions
Option4: It does not matter — both options result in the same session experience

QuestionText: A client configured a Datadog MCP server and a Knowledge note that says 'Query Datadog when investigating performance issues.' Devin now queries Datadog even for simple bug fixes unrelated to performance. Analyze the issue.
Option1: The Knowledge note's scope trigger is too broad — it activates for all sessions rather than being scoped to performance-related tasks. The fix is to narrow the trigger (e.g., 'When investigating performance issues or incident alerts related to latency/throughput') so the guidance only activates in appropriate contexts
Option2: Devin is over-eager and the MCP server should be removed entirely
Option3: The Knowledge note text is too vague — it should list specific Datadog queries to run
Option4: Devin cannot distinguish between performance issues and bug fixes — this is a platform limitation

QuestionText: A team has been using Devin for 6 months but has not created any Playbooks — they rely on detailed per-session prompts. Analyze the long-term impact of this approach.
Option1: Without Playbooks: (1) methodology is not standardized — different engineers write different quality prompts for the same task type, (2) institutional knowledge is siloed in individuals' prompt history rather than shared, (3) campaigns cannot scale because there is no reusable methodology, (4) the continuous improvement cycle stalls because refinements die with each session instead of compounding. Playbooks convert per-session effort into compounding organizational capital
Option2: This is acceptable — Playbooks are a convenience feature, not a necessity
Option3: The only impact is slightly higher ACU cost from longer prompts
Option4: Without Playbooks, Devin cannot execute multi-step tasks and is limited to single actions

QuestionText: Analyze the security implications of using org-scoped secrets vs. requiring each user to provide their own credentials per session.
Option1: Org-scoped secrets (service accounts): centralized management, easy rotation, clear audit trail, decouple from individual identities, no risk of credential loss when team members leave. Per-user credentials: more granular attribution but create management overhead, risk of orphaned access when people leave, and inconsistent access across sessions. Org-scoped with service accounts is architecturally preferable for team-operated agents
Option2: Per-user credentials are always more secure because they follow the principle of individual accountability
Option3: Org-scoped secrets are a security risk because one compromise affects all sessions
Option4: The security implications are identical — it is purely an administrative convenience choice

QuestionText: Analyze the relationship between MCP server availability and Devin's ability to handle tasks. If the Jira MCP server is temporarily unavailable, what happens?
Option1: Devin can still execute coding tasks normally — MCP integrations extend capabilities but are not required for core functionality (code, test, PR). Tasks that specifically require Jira interaction (read ticket, update status) will fail gracefully, and Devin should escalate or skip the Jira-specific steps. This shows the importance of designing tasks with graceful degradation when integrations are unavailable
Option2: All sessions fail because MCP server availability is a hard prerequisite for session startup
Option3: Devin automatically switches to using Jira's REST API directly instead of MCP
Option4: The session pauses and resumes when MCP becomes available again

QuestionText: Design a complete context layer configuration for a financial services client with: strict security requirements, 3 environments (dev/staging/prod), 50 developers across 4 teams, and compliance mandates for change traceability.
Option1: Blueprints: org-level (security tools, compliance scanners), repo-level per tech stack. Secrets: repo-scoped per environment (dev credentials separate from staging), no prod credentials in Devin. Knowledge: org-wide compliance requirements, team-specific domain knowledge with scoped triggers. Playbooks: compliance-aware templates (include traceability steps — link PR to ticket, include audit metadata). MCP: Jira (ticket tracking), security scanning tool. Git: service account with branch-protection-respecting permissions. Automations: PR events trigger compliance checks
Option2: Put all configuration in a single comprehensive org-level blueprint with inline Knowledge
Option3: Four Devin organizations (one per team) with completely independent configuration
Option4: Minimal configuration — let Devin figure out compliance requirements from the codebase

QuestionText: Design a Knowledge note governance strategy for an organization with 100+ notes. Include: creation criteria, ownership, review cadence, and deprecation process.
Option1: Creation criteria: note must apply to recurring scenarios (not one-off tasks), be verifiable/actionable, and not duplicate existing notes. Ownership: each note has a team owner responsible for accuracy. Review cadence: quarterly audit to identify stale/contradicting notes. Deprecation: notes not triggered in 3 months are flagged for review → owner confirms relevance or archives. Scope triggers reviewed during team onboarding to ensure new repos are covered
Option2: No governance needed — Knowledge notes are self-managing because Devin ignores irrelevant ones
Option3: Delete all notes annually and recreate only the ones teams remember as important
Option4: Assign one person as 'Knowledge administrator' who approves all note creation and modifications

QuestionText: Design an MCP server integration strategy for a client using: Jira for tickets, Confluence for docs, Datadog for monitoring, GitHub for code, and Slack for communication. Define what each integration enables.
Option1: Jira MCP: read ticket details/acceptance criteria for implementation context, update ticket status on PR creation. Confluence MCP: read existing architecture docs for context, update docs as part of implementation. Datadog MCP: query logs/metrics during incident investigation or performance optimization. GitHub: native Git connections for code access. Slack MCP: receive trigger messages, post status updates, escalate issues. All configured at org level with appropriate credentials as org secrets
Option2: Configure only Jira and GitHub — other integrations add unnecessary complexity
Option3: Configure all integrations but restrict each to specific teams rather than org-wide
Option4: Use browser automation to access all tools instead of MCP — it is more flexible

QuestionText: Design the rollout strategy for configuring Devin in an organization with no existing automation. Define phases and success criteria for each phase.
Option1: Phase 1 (Foundation, Week 1-2): Git connections + secrets + basic blueprint for one team's primary repo. Success: sessions can clone, build, and test. Phase 2 (Intelligence, Week 3-4): Knowledge notes for standards + MCP integrations. Success: sessions produce output matching team conventions. Phase 3 (Automation, Month 2): First playbook + first event-driven trigger. Success: recurring task runs without manual invocation. Phase 4 (Scale, Month 3+): Campaign architecture, additional teams. Success: multi-repo campaigns complete with >90% first-pass success
Option2: Configure everything on day 1 — there is no benefit to phased rollout
Option3: Start with Playbooks and campaigns immediately — infrastructure configuration is boring and can wait
Option4: Phase 1: Train all 100 engineers. Phase 2: Let them configure their own environments. Phase 3: Standardize retroactively

QuestionText: Design a secret rotation strategy for a Devin organization. Consider: how rotation affects running sessions, scheduled automations, and multi-team access.
Option1: (1) Secrets are referenced by name ($SECRET_NAME), not value — rotation updates the stored value without changing any configuration. (2) Running sessions: use the value injected at session start (rotation mid-session does not affect active work). (3) New sessions after rotation: automatically get the new value. (4) Strategy: rotate on a schedule, test with a canary session after rotation to verify the new credential works, then confirm all subsequent sessions function correctly. Multi-team: org-scoped secrets rotate once for everyone; repo-scoped secrets rotate independently per repo
Option2: Rebuild all blueprints and restart all scheduled automations after every rotation
Option3: Avoid rotation — secrets are safe because they are never persisted in session state
Option4: Create duplicate secrets (SECRET_V1, SECRET_V2) and manually switch references during rotation

QuestionText: Evaluate the statement: 'The upfront investment in context configuration pays dividends across every subsequent session.' Under what circumstances might this NOT be true?
Option1: The statement might not hold if: (1) the team's tech stack changes frequently (configurations become stale before paying off), (2) the organization runs very few sessions (the investment has too few sessions to amortize over), or (3) Knowledge notes and playbooks are written for one-off tasks rather than recurring patterns. The ROI depends on session volume × configuration reuse frequency
Option2: This is always true regardless of circumstances — any configuration is better than none
Option3: It would not be true only if the organization disbands within a month of configuration
Option4: It is not true for small teams (under 5 engineers) because they can remember standards without Knowledge notes

QuestionText: A client asks: 'Should we encode ALL team knowledge into Devin's Knowledge notes?' Evaluate this approach.
Option1: No — encode knowledge that is: (1) actionable for Devin (coding standards, workflow rules, architectural constraints), (2) recurring (applies to many sessions), and (3) not obvious from the code itself. Do NOT encode: tribal knowledge that requires human judgment, one-time decisions, or information that changes weekly. Over-encoding creates noise and maintenance burden. Target the knowledge that produces the highest quality improvement per note
Option2: Yes — more Knowledge notes always leads to better Devin output since it has more context
Option3: Only encode knowledge that has been documented in Confluence first
Option4: Encode nothing — Devin should learn conventions by reading the codebase directly

QuestionText: Evaluate the trade-off between configuring Devin extensively (30+ Knowledge notes, complex blueprints, many MCP servers) vs. a minimal configuration (basic blueprint, 5 Knowledge notes, Git only).
Option1: It depends on the organization's maturity and session volume. Extensive configuration yields higher per-session quality but requires maintenance effort. Minimal configuration has lower maintenance but produces less consistent output. The optimum is progressive: start minimal, add configuration as recurring patterns emerge (the continuous improvement cycle). Over-configuration without usage data leads to irrelevant notes; under-configuration at high volume wastes ACUs on avoidable mistakes
Option2: Extensive is always better — more configuration can never harm session quality
Option3: Minimal is always better — simpler systems are more maintainable and robust
Option4: The choice does not matter because Devin's AI adapts to any configuration level

QuestionText: An organization that invests heavily in Playbooks but neglects Knowledge notes and environment blueprints has a well-balanced context layer configuration.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Devin's clean-room execution model guarantees that each session is isolated from other sessions with no lateral movement risk between them.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: How does Devin's platform handle secrets to prevent credential exposure?
Option1: Secrets are injected at runtime via the platform's secrets management layer, never embedded in prompts or code, never persisted in session state, and scoped by org/user/repo level
Option2: Secrets are encrypted and stored in the Git repository alongside code
Option3: Secrets are stored in the VM snapshot so they persist across sessions
Option4: Secrets are available only through browser-based password manager access

QuestionText: What provides the audit trail for Devin-generated changes?
Option1: Every session has a full log of actions, decisions, and outputs — combined with the PR history, CI results, and merge approvals — creating end-to-end traceability from task to production
Option2: A dedicated compliance database that records all AI decisions
Option3: Email notifications sent to the security team for each change
Option4: Manual sign-off forms completed by the engineering manager

QuestionText: Why does the architecture specify 'service account friendly' credential management rather than using individual developer tokens?
Option1: Service accounts decouple agent access from individual identities — they provide least-privilege access scoped to what the agent needs, survive team member offboarding, and create clear audit separation between human and agent actions
Option2: Service accounts are free while developer tokens have per-seat licensing costs
Option3: Individual developer tokens would give Devin too much access to production systems
Option4: The platform only supports service account authentication, not OAuth tokens

QuestionText: Explain how the 'PR as review gate' pattern serves a compliance function.
Option1: The PR creates an auditable record of: what was proposed (diff), who reviewed it (approvers), what automated checks passed (CI/status checks), and who authorized the merge (committer). This satisfies change management requirements without additional compliance tooling
Option2: PRs are compliance-approved because they use encryption for all data in transit
Option3: The PR serves compliance by automatically generating regulatory reports
Option4: PRs satisfy compliance because they prevent any code from running in production

QuestionText: Devin's ephemeral testing environments ensure that no persistent state leaks between sessions, meaning test data from one session cannot contaminate another session's execution.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: A regulated client requires that Devin never has access to production customer data. How should the architect configure the platform to enforce this?
Option1: Never provision production database credentials as Devin secrets. Use repo-scoped secrets with only development/test environment access. Configure blueprints with synthetic test data or anonymized datasets. Network isolation ensures Devin's VM cannot reach production endpoints even if credentials were somehow available
Option2: Add a Knowledge note that says 'Never access production data'
Option3: Trust Devin to only access data relevant to the task — it has good judgment
Option4: Provision production read-only access so Devin can debug issues without modifying data

QuestionText: A client needs to meet SOC 2 requirements for change management. How does the architect map Devin's workflow to SOC 2 controls?
Option1: Map: session logs → evidence of authorized changes; PR with CI checks → separation of duties (agent proposes, human approves); branch protection rules → access control; session audit trail → monitoring and logging. Configure required reviewers on branches and status checks to enforce controls architecturally, not procedurally
Option2: Disable all Devin automations and require manual session creation with manager approval for each task
Option3: Add a compliance approval step before every Devin session launches
Option4: Configure Devin to generate SOC 2 compliance reports after each session

QuestionText: A client in healthcare needs HIPAA compliance. They want Devin to maintain their patient portal application. Design the security configuration.
Option1: No PHI in Devin's environment: synthetic test data only, no production database access, no production logs containing patient data. Secrets: development/test environment credentials only (repo-scoped). Network: VM cannot route to production VPC. Knowledge notes: HIPAA-specific coding standards (encryption requirements, audit logging patterns). Verification: SAST rules for PHI exposure patterns included in the test suite
Option2: Give Devin access to a de-identified copy of the production database for realistic testing
Option3: Configure data loss prevention (DLP) scanning on Devin's outbound network traffic
Option4: HIPAA compliance is not achievable with AI agents — advise the client against using Devin for this application

QuestionText: A client needs Devin to access their private cloud resources that sit behind a VPN. Design the secure network access architecture.
Option1: Options by security posture: (1) PrivateLink/VPC peering for cloud-to-cloud connectivity (most secure, no public internet traversal). (2) VPN configuration in the blueprint's maintenance block with credentials in secrets. (3) Allowlist Devin's static egress IPs on the client's firewall for specific endpoints. Choose based on client's cloud topology and security requirements. All approaches keep credentials in the platform's secrets layer, never in code or prompts
Option2: Open the client's firewall to all inbound traffic from any IP so Devin can connect from its dynamic IPs
Option3: Set up a publicly accessible bastion host that Devin connects through with SSH
Option4: Mirror all private resources to a public cloud endpoint that Devin can access directly

QuestionText: A client wants to ensure that if a Devin session is compromised (e.g., malicious prompt injection), the blast radius is minimized. What architectural controls should the architect implement?
Option1: (1) Least-privilege secret scoping (only credentials needed for the target repo). (2) Repo-scoped rather than org-scoped secrets where possible. (3) Branch protection preventing direct pushes to main. (4) Required human review before merge. (5) Network isolation limiting which endpoints the VM can reach. (6) Separate service account for agent actions (easy to revoke without affecting humans). These layers ensure a compromised session cannot access unrelated systems or bypass review gates
Option2: Real-time monitoring of all session activity with automatic termination on suspicious behavior
Option3: Require multi-factor authentication for every Devin API call
Option4: Run all Devin sessions in a secure air-gapped environment with no internet access

QuestionText: Analyze the security difference between Devin's 'secrets injected at runtime' model vs. storing credentials in .env files committed to repos.
Option1: Runtime injection: secrets exist only in the session's ephemeral memory, never in code/version control, never in snapshots, centrally managed and rotatable, scoped by access level. .env in repos: secrets are in version history forever (even if deleted), available to anyone with repo access, hard to rotate (must update every copy), and violate the principle of separating credentials from code
Option2: Both are equally secure since .env files are gitignored by convention
Option3: Runtime injection is less secure because secrets pass through the network
Option4: .env files are more secure because they are encrypted at rest by the filesystem

QuestionText: Analyze the governance implications of Devin's 'never merges its own PRs' principle. What governance gap would exist if this principle were violated?
Option1: If Devin could merge its own PRs: (1) no separation of duties between change creator and approver, (2) no human quality gate for AI-generated code, (3) no opportunity for architectural review, (4) compliance frameworks requiring human authorization would be violated, (5) incorrect or malicious code could reach production without human oversight. The principle is a fundamental governance control, not a convenience preference
Option2: The only gap would be missing attribution — it would be unclear who approved the change for audit purposes
Option3: There would be no governance gap because CI checks serve as the approval mechanism
Option4: The gap would only matter for production deployments, not feature branch merges

QuestionText: A security auditor asks: 'How do you know Devin is not exfiltrating code or secrets to external systems?' Analyze the platform's architectural safeguards.
Option1: (1) Session isolation means each VM is sandboxed with configurable network policies. (2) Full session logs provide transparency into every action taken. (3) Network egress can be restricted to known endpoints (Git host, CI, configured MCP servers). (4) Secrets are injected and used but never printed to logs. (5) The PR is the only output mechanism visible to the team — any code changes go through the review gate. Organizations can add network monitoring on the VM's egress for additional assurance
Option2: Devin's AI is trusted and would never exfiltrate data — the model is aligned to be safe
Option3: Code exfiltration is prevented by running all sessions on air-gapped networks
Option4: The platform scans all outbound network traffic in real-time for sensitive patterns

QuestionText: Analyze how Devin's Knowledge bus factor reduction claim works: 'Knowledge bus factor decreases when institutional knowledge is encoded in playbooks and knowledge notes rather than residing in individual engineers' heads.'
Option1: When a critical engineer leaves, their institutional knowledge (deployment procedures, debugging techniques, architecture decisions) is typically lost. If that knowledge is encoded in Knowledge notes and Playbooks, it persists in the platform's shared context layer — accessible to both human team members and Devin sessions. This reduces the risk of knowledge loss from attrition, making the team more resilient
Option2: Bus factor decreases because Devin replaces the need for multiple engineers
Option3: This only applies if the departing engineer was the one who wrote the Knowledge notes
Option4: Knowledge bus factor is a theoretical concern — in practice, knowledge is always duplicated across team members naturally

QuestionText: A client asks: 'If Devin has access to our Git repos and can push branches, what prevents it from modifying branch protection rules or security policies?' Analyze the access control model.
Option1: Devin uses service account credentials with least-privilege access — the service account should be configured with code push permissions only, NOT admin permissions. Branch protection rules, repository settings, and security policies are admin-level controls that a properly scoped service account cannot modify. The architect must ensure the Git service account does not have admin/owner role on repositories
Option2: The platform prevents this through hardcoded restrictions in the agent logic regardless of credential permissions
Option3: Knowledge notes tell Devin to never modify security settings, and it follows these instructions
Option4: This is prevented by the PR review process — any security policy change would require human approval

QuestionText: Analyze the compliance value of Devin's 'reproducible from scratch' architecture property. How does it support audit requirements?
Option1: Reproducibility means any session can be recreated from the same inputs (blueprint + prompt + context) to verify what happened. Auditors can: (1) confirm the environment was not tampered with (standard snapshot), (2) verify that the same inputs produce the same outputs (deterministic infrastructure), (3) trace the exact conditions under which a change was made. This supports evidence requirements for change management audits
Option2: Reproducibility has no compliance value — it is purely an operational convenience
Option3: Reproducibility means all Devin output can be replayed like a video recording for auditors
Option4: Reproducibility only matters for incident forensics, not routine compliance audits

QuestionText: A client's compliance team is concerned about 'AI decision-making in regulated code changes.' Analyze how Devin's architecture addresses this concern.
Option1: Devin proposes changes but NEVER makes the final decision to ship — the human merge approval is the decision point. The architecture separates execution (agent: write code, run tests, open PR) from authorization (human: review, approve, merge). Compliance requirements for human authorization are satisfied by the mandatory review gate. The AI makes implementation decisions; humans make release decisions
Option2: AI decision-making is not a concern because Devin follows deterministic rules, not AI judgment
Option3: The concern is valid and Devin should not be used for regulated code — human engineers only
Option4: Devin's session logs serve as the 'human decision record' since they show the reasoning process

QuestionText: Analyze the data residency implications of Devin's architecture. A European client subject to GDPR asks where their code and data reside during sessions.
Option1: Sessions run on VMs in specific regions — the architect must verify that the session execution region aligns with the client's data residency requirements. Code is processed in the VM (region-specific), secrets are injected at runtime (not persisted outside the session), and session logs are stored in the platform (region policies apply). The key architectural question is whether the VM region, platform storage, and any MCP transit paths all comply with the client's residency requirements
Option2: Code never leaves the client's Git server — Devin accesses it remotely without downloading
Option3: GDPR does not apply to source code since it is not personal data
Option4: All Devin sessions run in the US regardless of client location

QuestionText: Design a security governance framework for a Devin deployment that satisfies: least-privilege access, separation of duties, change traceability, and incident response capability.
Option1: Least-privilege: repo-scoped secrets, service accounts with code-only permissions, no prod access. Separation of duties: agent implements (session) → automated checks verify (CI) → human authorizes (PR approval) — three distinct actors. Traceability: event trigger → session log → PR → CI results → merge commit → deploy pipeline — linked chain of evidence. Incident response: event-driven automation for triage + escalation path to human when agent cannot resolve. All enforced architecturally through platform configuration, not procedurally through documentation
Option2: A written security policy document that all engineers must acknowledge before using Devin
Option3: Real-time monitoring dashboard with alerts when Devin accesses sensitive files
Option4: Quarterly security audits of all Devin sessions by an external compliance firm

QuestionText: Design a compliance-aware automation architecture where every Devin-initiated change includes: trigger provenance, automated security scan, human approval record, and deployment tracking.
Option1: Trigger event (logged with source ID) → Devin session (linked to trigger) → implementation → SAST/DAST scan as part of CI → PR (contains: trigger reference, session URL, scan results as status checks) → human review + approval (recorded in PR history) → merge triggers deployment pipeline (linked back to PR) → deployment record includes full provenance chain from trigger to production. Knowledge note ensures Devin includes traceability metadata in every PR description
Option2: Add a compliance sign-off form that humans fill out after each Devin PR is merged
Option3: Configure Devin Review to flag any PR missing compliance metadata and block the merge
Option4: Create a separate compliance tracking system outside of Git that mirrors all PR data

QuestionText: Design a network security architecture for Devin sessions that need access to: (1) GitHub (public), (2) internal Artifactory (private VPC), and (3) client Jira (corporate network behind ZPA). Balance security with functionality.
Option1: GitHub: direct HTTPS access (public endpoint, no special configuration). Artifactory: configure blueprint with VPC connectivity (PrivateLink or VPN to client's AWS VPC where Artifactory lives, credentials in secrets). Jira: MCP server configured to route through ZPA connector (zero-trust access, no broad VPN needed). Each path is independently secured — failure of one connectivity method does not expose others. Network policies restrict VM egress to only these three destinations
Option2: Configure a single VPN that provides access to all three destinations simultaneously
Option3: Use a forward proxy for all outbound connections that logs and filters all traffic
Option4: Give the VM a public IP and configure security groups to allow all outbound traffic

QuestionText: Design an access control model for a Devin deployment where: development team can trigger sessions for any repo, security team can trigger SAST remediation campaigns, and management can view session status but not trigger sessions.
Option1: Organizational RBAC: (1) Development team: session creation + repository access for their repos + secret access (repo-scoped). (2) Security team: session creation + cross-repo campaign permissions + SAST tool credentials (org-scoped for scanning). (3) Management: read-only access to session status, campaign dashboards, and PR outcomes — no session creation or secret access. Service accounts per role with appropriate scope. Branch protection prevents any role from bypassing the review gate
Option2: Single shared account for all roles with different Knowledge notes per team
Option3: Separate Devin organizations per role with cross-org visibility configured
Option4: All users get full access and trust organizational policy to prevent misuse

QuestionText: Design a secret lifecycle management process for Devin that covers: provisioning, injection, rotation, revocation, and emergency response.
Option1: Provisioning: create dedicated service accounts with minimal permissions, store in platform secrets with appropriate scope. Injection: platform injects at session start (never in code/logs). Rotation: scheduled rotation with canary session to verify new credentials work. Revocation: immediately update platform secret value (all new sessions get revoked credential = graceful degradation, not continued access). Emergency: if compromise suspected, revoke service account at the provider level (instant, affects all sessions), then provision new credentials. Audit: log all secret access and rotation events
Option2: Store all secrets in a HashiCorp Vault and configure each session to authenticate independently
Option3: Embed secrets in the blueprint so they are baked into the snapshot for fastest access
Option4: Use short-lived tokens that expire every 15 minutes and are auto-renewed by the platform

QuestionText: A CISO states: 'AI agents writing code is inherently riskier than human engineers writing code.' Evaluate this claim in the context of Devin's architecture.
Option1: The claim is nuanced: AI agents introduce different risks (potential for pattern-based mistakes at scale), but Devin's architecture adds controls that human development often lacks — mandatory CI gates, automated security scans, full session audit trails, and enforced review. The net risk depends on configuration quality. A well-configured Devin with proper review gates may produce lower-risk changes than a human developer who skips tests or self-merges
Option2: The claim is correct — AI is less reliable than humans and introduces more security vulnerabilities
Option3: The claim is incorrect — AI produces more consistent and therefore safer code than humans
Option4: The claim is irrelevant because human reviewers catch all AI-introduced vulnerabilities

QuestionText: Evaluate the security trade-off of giving Devin access to observability tools (Datadog, logs) for incident response vs. restricting all data access.
Option1: Read-only observability access enables automated incident triage (reducing MTTR) without write access to production systems. The risk is exposure to potentially sensitive log data. Mitigation: configure log queries to exclude PII fields, use the MCP server to control which queries are possible, and ensure the observability credentials are read-only. The security posture is acceptable when the MTTR reduction outweighs the controlled data exposure risk, and logs are already accessible to the broader engineering team
Option2: Never give Devin access to any production data including logs — the security risk is never justified
Option3: Give full read-write access to all observability tools — the agent needs complete context to be effective
Option4: Only allow log access during incident hours and revoke immediately after resolution

QuestionText: Evaluate the governance model where Devin Review is used as a first-pass reviewer before human review. Does this create compliance issues or resolve them?
Option1: It resolves compliance concerns by adding a quality layer: Devin Review catches bugs, security issues, and style problems before human review, making human reviewers more effective. It does NOT replace human approval for compliance purposes — the human is still the final authority. This layered approach (automated first-pass + human final approval) exceeds the governance requirements of human-only review for most frameworks
Option2: It creates compliance issues because an AI system is making review decisions about code
Option3: It resolves all compliance concerns and eliminates the need for human review
Option4: It is governance-neutral — Devin Review has no impact on the compliance posture

QuestionText: A client proposes: 'We will trust Devin fully — no human review required for changes to non-production branches and test files.' Evaluate this relaxation of controls.
Option1: This is a reasonable risk-based approach for LOW-risk artifacts (test files, documentation, dev-only configs) where incorrect changes have no production impact and are easily reversible. However, the architect should still maintain: CI must pass, SAST must pass, and auto-merge should be limited to specific file patterns. This is a graduated trust model — not blanket removal of all controls
Option2: This is unacceptable — every change must be human-reviewed regardless of risk level
Option3: This is ideal — extend it to all code changes to maximize velocity
Option4: This only works if Devin Review is configured as the replacement reviewer for these changes

QuestionText: Evaluate whether Devin's architecture satisfies the 'defense in depth' principle for preventing unauthorized code from reaching production.
Option1: Yes — multiple independent layers: (1) session isolation (attacker in one session cannot affect others), (2) branch protection (cannot push directly to main), (3) CI checks (automated quality gates), (4) SAST/security scans (vulnerability detection), (5) Devin Review (automated bug detection), (6) human review (architectural judgment), (7) merge approval (human authorization). Compromising one layer does not bypass the others. This is textbook defense in depth
Option2: No — Devin can bypass all layers by pushing directly to main since it has Git access
Option3: Partially — the layers exist but they all depend on the same platform, creating a single point of failure
Option4: Yes, but only if all seven layers are configured — most organizations skip several

QuestionText: An architect who configures Devin with org-wide admin-level GitHub credentials to 'make things simpler' is following security best practices because it reduces configuration complexity.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: A client with 80 microservices wants: automated security remediation + quarterly framework upgrades + incident response + team onboarding. Each service has different tech stacks. Apply the platform patterns to design the initial architecture.
Option1: Security: event-driven (SAST webhook → per-repo child sessions). Upgrades: quarterly campaign (parent-child, batched by tech stack group, playbook per stack). Incident: event-driven (alert → single session with MCP access to logs). Onboarding: campaign playbook for new repos (blueprint + Knowledge generation). All unified by: repo-scoped secrets per service, tech-stack-grouped blueprints, shared org-level Knowledge for cross-cutting standards
Option2: One comprehensive playbook that handles all four concerns for any service
Option3: 80 separate Devin configurations, one per service, each handling all four concerns
Option4: Hire a dedicated DevOps team to manually coordinate Devin for each concern

QuestionText: A client is migrating from a monolith to microservices while simultaneously modernizing from .NET Framework to .NET 8. Both efforts must proceed in parallel without blocking each other. Design the coordination architecture.
Option1: Two parallel workstreams with independent branches: (1) Modernization: sequential single-agent sessions that upgrade the monolith in-place (.NET Framework → .NET 8) on a modernization branch. (2) Decomposition: sequential extraction sessions that carve services from the modernized monolith. Coordination: modernization branch must be stable before decomposition starts extracting from it — this creates a handoff dependency. Both workstreams share Knowledge notes about architecture decisions. Result: modernize first, then decompose — not truly parallel for the same code, but parallel planning is viable
Option2: Spawn 80 child agents to simultaneously extract microservices AND upgrade .NET in one pass
Option3: Do both transformations in a single branch to avoid merge complexity
Option4: Fully parallelize both workstreams with different Devin organizations — merge at the end

QuestionText: A client needs a complete Devin architecture for their DevSecOps pipeline: code → build → test → scan → deploy staging → validate → deploy prod. Design where Devin participates at each stage.
Option1: Code: Devin implements tasks (event/manual trigger) → Build: blueprint ensures local build capability, Devin verifies before pushing → Test: Devin runs unit tests locally, monitors CI for integration tests → Scan: SAST runs in CI, findings trigger remediation sessions → Deploy staging: CD pipeline (not Devin) deploys merged code → Validate: event-driven Devin session runs smoke tests against staging → Deploy prod: human approval gate in CD pipeline. Devin touches code/test/scan/validate but never directly deploys
Option2: Devin handles the entire pipeline end-to-end including production deployment
Option3: Devin only participates in the code and test phases — everything else is handled by existing CI/CD
Option4: Devin is a replacement for the CD pipeline and handles all deployment logic

QuestionText: A financial services client needs: real-time fraud rule updates (within 4 hours of detection), zero customer data exposure to the agent, full audit trail, and multi-team review (security + engineering). Analyze which platform patterns combine to satisfy all four requirements simultaneously.
Option1: 4-hour SLA: event-driven trigger from fraud detection system → immediate session. Zero data exposure: repo-scoped secrets (no prod access), synthetic test data in blueprint. Audit trail: session log + PR + CI + merge record = full chain. Multi-team review: PR requires approvals from both security team and engineering team (branch protection rules with multiple required reviewers from different teams). All patterns are composable and independently satisfied
Option2: A dedicated Devin session that continuously monitors the fraud system and pushes changes in real-time
Option3: The requirements are contradictory — fast response (4 hours) cannot coexist with multi-team review
Option4: Parent-child pattern with security team's child reviewing engineering team's child output

QuestionText: A platform team supports 15 product teams. They want Devin to: maintain shared infrastructure (IaC), respond to cross-cutting incidents, enforce platform standards, and provide self-service tooling. Analyze the organizational architecture needed.
Option1: Platform team owns: org-level blueprint (shared infra tools), org-level Knowledge (platform standards), cross-cutting automation rules (incidents, compliance). Product teams own: repo-level blueprints (their tech stacks), team-scoped Knowledge (domain conventions), team-specific playbooks. Automation routing: platform incidents → platform team's automation; product incidents → respective product team's automation. Shared MCP servers for observability. This creates appropriate ownership boundaries while sharing the org-level foundation
Option2: Platform team manages all Devin configuration; product teams only consume sessions without any configuration authority
Option3: Each product team has their own Devin org; the platform team has a 16th org for infrastructure only
Option4: One flat configuration where all teams see all Knowledge, all playbooks, all secrets

QuestionText: A client is implementing Devin across three locations (US, EU, APAC) with data residency requirements. EU code must not be processed in US data centers. Analyze the architectural constraints.
Option1: The architecture must ensure: EU repository sessions run on VMs in EU regions, secrets for EU services are scoped to EU repos only, and session logs for EU sessions are stored within EU boundaries. This requires: verifying platform region capabilities, configuring geo-aware automation rules, and potentially separate session routing for different repo groups. The org-level vs. repo-level configuration hierarchy naturally supports this via repo-scoped settings
Option2: Data residency is not a concern for code since source code is not personal data under GDPR
Option3: Use a single US region for all processing but encrypt everything in transit
Option4: Create three separate Devin organizations (one per region) with no shared configuration

QuestionText: An organization runs both greenfield (new services) and brownfield (legacy maintenance) work. Analyze how the Devin architecture should differ between these two modes.
Option1: Greenfield: single-agent sessions with detailed architecture guidance in Knowledge, humans drive design decisions, Devin implements after human planning phase. Brownfield: event-driven/scheduled sessions for maintenance, campaigns for modernization, more prescriptive playbooks since patterns are established. Key difference: greenfield needs more human-in-the-loop at design phase; brownfield benefits from higher automation with established patterns. Both share the same platform but use different automation maturity levels
Option2: No difference — the same configuration works for both greenfield and brownfield
Option3: Greenfield should not use Devin because AI cannot do novel architecture design
Option4: Brownfield should use a completely different platform because legacy code requires specialized tools

QuestionText: A client wants Devin to support both their inner-loop development (quick iterations) and outer-loop operations (CI/CD, deployments, monitoring). Analyze how the two-tier model (cloud + local agents) maps to these loops.
Option1: Inner loop (fast feedback, interactive): Local agents (Devin CLI/Desktop) for exploration, prototyping, quick fixes — developer stays in the loop. Outer loop (autonomous, background): Cloud Devin for CI monitoring, event-driven responses, scheduled maintenance, campaigns — developer delegates and reviews results. The /handoff command bridges the two: start locally (inner loop), delegate to cloud (outer loop). This maps each tier to its natural strength
Option2: Both loops should use cloud Devin exclusively for consistency
Option3: Inner loop should use local agents exclusively; outer loop should use traditional CI/CD without Devin
Option4: The two-tier model does not map to inner/outer loops — both tiers serve the same purpose

QuestionText: A large enterprise has 500 engineers across 30 teams with diverse maturity levels — some teams use CI/CD extensively while others deploy manually. Analyze the rollout strategy that maximizes adoption across this heterogeneity.
Option1: Stratified rollout: (1) Start with mature teams who already have CI/CD — they benefit immediately from event-driven patterns and campaigns. (2) Use their success to build playbooks and configuration templates. (3) Roll out to mid-maturity teams with the proven templates — they get value from automated maintenance without needing full CI/CD. (4) Finally, help low-maturity teams use Devin to build their CI/CD and testing infrastructure. Each tier's success provides patterns for the next. Shared org-level configuration provides a foundation for all tiers
Option2: Force all 500 engineers to adopt Devin simultaneously with a single training session
Option3: Only onboard teams that already have full CI/CD maturity — skip the rest
Option4: Start with the least mature teams since they have the most to gain from automation

QuestionText: A client's architecture includes: microservices (Java/Kotlin), a data platform (Python/Spark), mobile apps (Swift/Kotlin), and shared libraries (TypeScript). Each domain has different testing philosophies, deployment cadences, and team cultures. Analyze how to design a unified Devin architecture that respects these differences.
Option1: Unified org-level foundation: Git connections, shared security standards (Knowledge), common tools (Docker). Domain-specific configuration: separate repo-level blueprints per tech stack, domain-scoped Knowledge notes (microservices testing patterns vs. data pipeline validation vs. mobile UI testing), domain-specific playbooks. Automation topology: different trigger sensitivities per domain (microservices: immediate CI response; data: scheduled pipeline validation; mobile: on-release testing). The architecture is hierarchical: org → domain → repo
Option2: One large blueprint and playbook set that works for all domains simultaneously
Option3: Four completely separate Devin organizations with no shared configuration
Option4: Standardize all teams onto one tech stack first, then configure Devin — heterogeneity is the real problem

QuestionText: Design a complete Devin architecture for a startup that is scaling from 5 to 50 engineers over 12 months. The architecture must grow with the team without requiring re-architecture at each stage.
Option1: Foundation (now, 5 engineers): Git + secrets + one repo blueprint + basic Knowledge. Growth (month 3-6, 15 engineers): team-scoped Knowledge, playbooks for recurring tasks, first event-driven automation, MCP integrations. Scale (month 6-12, 50 engineers): campaign capability, multi-team Knowledge governance, comprehensive automation topology, ACU budgets per team. Key design: use scope triggers and repo-level configuration from the start — these naturally partition as teams form without migration. Avoid org-level overloading that would need restructuring later
Option2: Build the full 50-engineer architecture on day 1 to avoid future re-work
