Option3: No architecture needed — startups should use Devin ad-hoc until they are large enough to justify structure
Option4: Start with a single all-encompassing Devin session that handles all tasks for the 5-person team

QuestionText: Design a disaster recovery architecture for Devin automation. If the primary automation system fails (webhooks stop, scheduled sessions do not fire), how should the client maintain continuity?
Option1: (1) Monitoring: health check on automation endpoints — alert when webhooks are not received within expected SLA. (2) Dead letter queue: failed webhook deliveries are stored and replayed when service recovers. (3) Manual fallback: engineering team can trigger sessions directly via UI/API for urgent work. (4) Idempotent design: when automation recovers, replaying queued events does not create duplicate sessions. (5) Stateless triggers: no automation state that cannot be reconstructed from event sources. This ensures no work is lost and the system self-heals
Option2: Run two Devin organizations in active-active mode and failover between them
Option3: Accept that automation failures mean manual work until the system recovers — no design needed
Option4: Keep a dedicated engineer on-call whose job is to manually trigger Devin sessions if automation fails

QuestionText: Design an architecture that supports a client's requirement for 'AI-assisted continuous delivery' — where Devin participates in every stage from commit to production deployment without removing human oversight.
Option1: Commit: developer pushes (or Devin session completes and pushes) → PR opened. Review: Devin Review (automated first-pass) + human review (approval gate). Build/Test: CI pipeline (not Devin). Scan: SAST in CI → findings trigger remediation sessions. Deploy staging: CD pipeline on merge. Validate staging: event-driven Devin session runs acceptance tests. Production gate: human approval in CD pipeline (Devin cannot approve). Deploy prod: CD pipeline. Monitor: Devin responds to post-deploy alerts. Each stage has clear human vs. agent vs. pipeline ownership
Option2: Devin manages the entire CD pipeline including production deployment decisions
Option3: Devin only participates in the commit and review stages — CD is out of scope for AI
Option4: Replace the CD pipeline entirely with Devin sessions that deploy using shell commands

QuestionText: Design a multi-tier testing architecture where Devin operates at: unit level (fast), integration level (medium), and system level (slow). Define what Devin does at each tier and how the tiers connect.
Option1: Unit tier (Devin VM, seconds): Devin runs unit tests locally for fast feedback before pushing — catches syntax, logic errors, and regressions immediately. Integration tier (CI, minutes): after push, CI runs integration tests — Devin monitors, reads failure logs, and iterates. System tier (staging, hours): after merge + deploy to staging, event-driven Devin session runs end-to-end tests against staging environment. Connection: each tier gates the next — unit must pass before push, integration before merge, system before production. Failures at any tier trigger Devin's iterate-or-escalate loop
Option2: Run all three test tiers on Devin's VM to minimize feedback loop time
Option3: Only run unit tests via Devin — integration and system testing are manual human activities
Option4: Run all tests in CI only — Devin should not run any tests locally to avoid environment drift

QuestionText: Design a complete automation topology for a SaaS company's operations: incident response, dependency management, security compliance, feature delivery, and technical debt reduction. Show how these interact.
Option1: Incident: alert webhook → immediate session (MCP: logs/metrics) → fix PR or escalation. Dependencies: weekly cron → parent-child campaign (per-service). Security: SAST webhook → remediation session per finding group. Features: Jira ticket trigger → implementation session. Tech debt: monthly cron → analysis parent → prioritized child sessions for top items. Interactions: security scans cover dependency PRs (catch vuln bumps), incident fixes may trigger tech debt items, feature implementations trigger Devin Review. All share org context layer. Priority: incidents pre-empt scheduled work (separate from batch sessions)
Option2: One comprehensive scheduled session that handles all five concerns every night
Option3: Five independent Devin configurations that never interact with each other
Option4: Only automate incident response and dependency management — the rest should remain manual

QuestionText: Design an architecture for a client migrating from monorepo to polyrepo. During the 6-month transition, Devin must support both structures simultaneously, with some code existing in both locations.
Option1: Phase 1: blueprint and Knowledge configured for monorepo. Phase 2 (transition): both blueprints active — monorepo sessions have Knowledge about which code is moving, polyrepo sessions have Knowledge about dependencies still in monorepo. Cross-reference Knowledge notes tell Devin where to find code during transition. Phase 3: deprecated monorepo references removed from Knowledge. Key: use Knowledge note scoping so monorepo sessions get monorepo guidance and polyrepo sessions get polyrepo guidance. Campaign to move automations from monorepo triggers to polyrepo triggers progressively
Option2: Complete the migration before configuring Devin — it cannot handle the transitional state
Option3: Configure Devin only for the polyrepo and ignore the monorepo during transition
Option4: Maintain duplicate configurations forever and let Devin figure out which to use

QuestionText: Design the complete platform architecture for an SI (Systems Integrator) that delivers Devin solutions to multiple clients. Each client has different security requirements, tech stacks, and automation maturity. The SI needs to: maintain reusable assets, isolate client data, and scale delivery.
Option1: Separate Devin organizations per client (data isolation). SI-internal org for developing reusable playbooks and templates. Client onboarding playbook: analyze client stack → configure org → create blueprints → seed Knowledge. Reusable assets: playbook library (framework upgrades, security remediation, migration patterns) copied into client orgs. Scaling: standardized onboarding process means new client delivery uses proven templates. Knowledge: SI institutional knowledge (engagement patterns) in SI org, client-specific knowledge in client orgs
Option2: One shared Devin organization for all clients with Knowledge notes to separate contexts
Option3: A custom Devin fork for each client's specific requirements
Option4: No shared assets — build everything from scratch per client engagement

QuestionText: Design a progressive trust model for Devin: from initial deployment (zero trust) to mature operation (high trust). Define what changes at each trust level and what criteria must be met to advance.
Option1: Level 1 (Zero trust): manual session creation only, human review of every PR, no automations. Advance when: 20+ sessions with >90% first-pass quality. Level 2 (Basic trust): event-driven triggers for low-risk tasks (dep bumps, doc updates), auto-merge for test-only changes. Advance when: 50+ automated sessions with no incidents. Level 3 (Operational trust): campaigns, scheduled maintenance, security remediation automation. Advance when: campaign success rate >95%. Level 4 (Strategic trust): complex multi-domain automations, incident response, cross-team campaigns. Each level requires demonstrated reliability at the previous level
Option2: Deploy full automation from day 1 — trust is built by exposure, not by limiting capabilities
Option3: Stay at zero trust permanently — AI agents should never be trusted with autonomous action
Option4: Trust levels should be per-team rather than organization-wide since teams have different risk tolerance

QuestionText: Design an architecture that handles a 'day 2' scenario: the initial Devin deployment is successful, but 6 months later the team has: 200 Knowledge notes (many stale), 50 automations (some conflicting), and session volume outpacing review capacity. Design the remediation architecture.
Option1: Knowledge hygiene: audit scope triggers, remove unused notes (not triggered in 3 months), consolidate overlapping notes, assign ownership. Automation cleanup: map all triggers to identify conflicts/redundancy, add rate limiting, deduplicate overlapping rules. Review scaling: add Devin Review as first-pass to reduce human review burden, implement concurrency limits on campaigns to match review capacity, prioritize automations by value (security > maintenance > docs). Governance: quarterly configuration review, ownership model, change approval for new automations
Option2: Delete everything and start over with a clean configuration
Option3: Accept the sprawl as the natural cost of automation at scale — no remediation needed
Option4: Reduce session volume by removing automations until review capacity matches

QuestionText: Design a cost optimization architecture for a client running 500+ Devin sessions per week. Identify where ACU waste occurs and how to reduce it without reducing output quality.
Option1: Waste sources and mitigations: (1) Blueprint optimization: bake deps into snapshot (eliminate install time per session). (2) Local testing before push (reduce CI iteration cycles from 3 to 1). (3) Knowledge notes for common mistakes (reduce rework iterations). (4) Playbook refinement (reduce failed sessions that produce no output). (5) Trigger filtering (eliminate low-value sessions). (6) Concurrency tuning (prevent sessions waiting for rate-limited resources). Measurement: track ACU-per-merged-PR as efficiency metric — improvements compound as configuration matures
Option2: Reduce the number of sessions by making each session handle more tasks sequentially
Option3: Switch to a cheaper AI model to reduce per-session ACU cost
Option4: Set hard ACU limits per session and terminate any session that exceeds the budget

QuestionText: Evaluate the architectural decision to use Devin for both routine maintenance AND strategic migrations. A colleague argues these should use different platforms. Who is correct?
Option1: Using one platform for both is architecturally sound — the shared context layer (Knowledge, blueprints, playbooks) creates compounding value across both use cases. Routine maintenance refines the configuration that migrations benefit from. Different platforms would require duplicate configuration, lose cross-pollination benefits, and increase operational complexity. The patterns (single agent, parent-child, event-driven, scheduled) naturally accommodate both modes
Option2: The colleague is correct — routine maintenance needs a simple scheduler while migrations need specialized tools
Option3: Neither is fully correct — maintenance should use Devin and migrations should use dedicated migration platforms (e.g., AWS Migration Hub)
Option4: The colleague is correct because mixing workload types on one platform creates resource contention

QuestionText: Evaluate a client's proposed architecture: 'We will use Devin exclusively for implementation — all design, testing strategy, and architecture decisions will be made by humans first, documented in Confluence, then handed to Devin as specifications.' Is this optimal?
Option1: This is a solid foundation (human design → agent implementation) but leaves value on the table. Devin can also assist with: research/analysis during design phase, test strategy execution (not just following a human-defined strategy), automated verification during implementation, and ongoing maintenance after initial delivery. The gray-zone pattern (agent research → human decision → agent implementation → human review) is more productive than pure 'design → implement' handoff
Option2: This is the optimal architecture — humans should always make all decisions and Devin should only execute
Option3: This is suboptimal — Devin should participate in design decisions using its codebase knowledge
Option4: This is unnecessary — just give Devin the requirements directly without the intermediate Confluence step

QuestionText: A client achieved 10x faster delivery using Devin campaigns but code review quality dropped because reviewers are overwhelmed. Evaluate whether this is a net positive or negative outcome.
Option1: Net negative if review quality drops below the threshold needed to catch production issues. The velocity gain is illusory if defects increase. Solution: recalibrate concurrency to review capacity, add Devin Review as first-pass (reduce human review burden), establish quality metrics (defect escape rate), and only increase campaign velocity to the point where review quality remains acceptable. True velocity = merged quality code, not just opened PRs
Option2: Net positive — faster delivery always outweighs review concerns since CI catches bugs automatically
Option3: Net negative — the team should revert to manual implementation to restore review quality
Option4: Net positive — reviewers will adapt to the higher volume over time without intervention

QuestionText: Evaluate the statement: 'The most mature Devin organization has zero manual session creation — everything is triggered by events or schedules.' Is this a reasonable maturity target?
Option1: This is not a reasonable target. Manual (ad-hoc) sessions serve essential purposes: novel feature work, exploratory research, one-off tasks, and creative work where the prompt comes from human judgment in the moment. The highest maturity is: routine/reactive work is automated, novel/strategic work is manually initiated. Zero manual creation would mean either over-automating creative work or not using Devin for it at all — both are suboptimal
Option2: This is the correct maturity target — full automation means maximum ROI from the platform
Option3: This is achievable but only for organizations that have completely automated their SDLC
Option4: This is reasonable for maintenance teams but not for feature development teams

QuestionText: Evaluate the ROI of investing 40 engineering hours in Devin configuration (blueprints, Knowledge, playbooks) for an organization that runs 50 sessions/week vs. one that runs 5 sessions/week.
Option1: 50 sessions/week: each configuration improvement amortizes across 200+ sessions/month — even a 2-minute reduction per session (from better blueprints) saves 400 minutes/month. The 40-hour investment pays back within 2-3 weeks. 5 sessions/week: same investment amortizes across only 20 sessions/month — payback takes 5-6 months. Configuration investment scales linearly with session volume. The 50-session org should invest aggressively; the 5-session org should start with high-impact items only (blueprint basics, critical Knowledge)
Option2: Both organizations benefit equally — configuration quality is volume-independent
Option3: Neither should invest 40 hours — configuration should be built incrementally over time, never as a focused investment
Option4: Only the 50-session org should use Devin at all — 5 sessions/week does not justify the platform

QuestionText: A client claims: 'We can achieve the same results by just writing better prompts — we do not need Knowledge notes, blueprints, or playbooks.' Evaluate this claim.
Option1: This fails at scale for three reasons: (1) better prompts are per-session effort that does not compound (every engineer must write the perfect prompt every time), (2) there is no consistency enforcement across team members (each writes differently), (3) critical context (standards, conventions, architecture decisions) must be re-stated in every prompt. The shared context layer converts per-session effort into one-time organizational investment — it is the difference between individual productivity and team-level scaling
Option2: The claim is valid — well-crafted prompts contain all necessary context without platform configuration
Option3: The claim is valid for small teams (under 10) but breaks down for larger organizations
Option4: Configuration only matters for campaigns — single-session tasks work fine with good prompts alone

QuestionText: Evaluate whether a local-only agent strategy (no cloud Devin) can achieve the same outcomes as the full platform for an enterprise team.
Option1: A local-only strategy cannot replicate: (1) persistence (sessions tied to terminal/IDE lifetime), (2) parallelism (cannot spawn child agents on separate VMs), (3) event-driven automation (local agents need a human present), (4) scheduled operations (no background cron), (5) organizational context layer (shared Knowledge, Playbooks, Secrets). Local agents accelerate individual developers but cannot provide team-level autonomous capacity. The enterprise value comes from the cloud tier's independence from human presence
Option2: Local agents can achieve the same outcomes with better developer experience since they are faster
Option3: A local-only strategy is actually superior because it avoids the security concerns of cloud execution
Option4: Local agents plus a CI/CD pipeline can replicate all cloud Devin capabilities

QuestionText: Evaluate the following trade-off: a client can either configure Devin thoroughly for their 5 most critical repos (deep investment, narrow scope) or configure it minimally for all 50 repos (shallow investment, broad scope). Which yields more value in the first 3 months?
Option1: Deep investment in 5 critical repos likely yields more value because: (1) high-traffic repos generate more sessions (more return per configuration hour), (2) thorough configuration produces higher quality output (fewer failed sessions, less rework), (3) success stories from well-configured repos demonstrate value and inform configuration templates for the remaining 45. Breadth without depth produces mediocre results everywhere and makes it harder to demonstrate clear ROI
Option2: Broad scope is always better — more repos means more total sessions means more total value
Option3: Both approaches yield identical value since the total configuration effort is the same
Option4: Neither — wait 3 months to observe usage patterns before investing in any configuration

QuestionText: A team reports: 'Devin works great for us individually but we do not see team-level productivity gains.' Evaluate what is likely missing from their architecture.
Option1: Individual success without team-level gains indicates the shared context layer is underutilized: likely missing Knowledge notes (standards are not codified → inconsistent output), missing Playbooks (methodologies are not shared → each person reinvents), missing automations (reactive/scheduled work still requires human initiation), and no campaigns (large-scale work still sequential). The platform's team-level value comes from configuration that compounds across sessions from all team members — not from individual session quality alone
Option2: Team-level gains are not achievable with AI tools — only individual productivity can improve
Option3: The team needs more training on writing effective prompts
Option4: They need to increase their ACU budget to enable more concurrent sessions

QuestionText: Evaluate the claim that 'Devin's continuous improvement cycle creates a competitive moat — organizations that invest early will compound advantages over those that start later.'
Option1: The claim is directionally correct: Knowledge notes, refined playbooks, and mature automation rules represent institutional capital that new adopters do not have. Each session teaches the organization something (via Knowledge refinement), and future sessions benefit from all prior learning. However, the moat is not insurmountable — latecomers can start with templates and accelerate their improvement cycle. The advantage is strongest in domain-specific knowledge and automation topology that is hard to replicate without operational experience
Option2: The claim is wrong — there is no compound advantage because all organizations start from the same platform capabilities
Option3: The claim is true and insurmountable — later adopters can never catch up to early movers
Option4: The compound advantage only exists for enterprises with 500+ engineers — small teams cannot build enough configuration capital

QuestionText: Devin scheduled sessions can use cron expressions for custom recurrence patterns beyond simple daily or weekly intervals.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: A client wants Devin to automatically investigate alerts from their PagerDuty monitoring system before waking the on-call engineer. Design the automation configuration.
Option1: PagerDuty webhook fires on alert → trigger layer validates and enriches with recent logs/metrics → Devin session investigates using MCP access to observability tools → if fix is straightforward, opens PR; if complex, posts analysis to incident channel for human triage. Safeguards: dedup by alert ID, max sessions per incident, exclude already-acknowledged alerts
Option2: Configure Devin to poll PagerDuty API every 30 seconds for new alerts
Option3: Give Devin access to PagerDuty's mobile app and have it respond to push notifications
Option4: Schedule a Devin session every hour that checks for recent alerts and investigates any it finds

QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'maximum retry count per trigger' safeguard in event-driven automation?
Option1: To prevent runaway sessions where a trigger keeps firing and creating endless sessions for the same unresolvable issue
Option2: To limit the total ACU spend per month
Option3: To ensure events are processed in the correct order
Option4: To prevent Devin from overwriting its own previous fixes

QuestionText: Why are event-driven Devin responses described as producing 'better outcomes than humans getting paged'?
Option1: Devin responds instantly (no human context-switch time), works 24/7 (no off-hours delay), processes systematically (no human fatigue or oversight), and investigates before escalating (humans get pre-analyzed context rather than raw alerts). The event-to-action latency drops from minutes/hours to seconds
Option2: Because humans make more mistakes when they are paged during sleep
Option3: Because Devin has better access to monitoring tools than human engineers
Option4: Because paging systems are unreliable and often fail to deliver alerts

QuestionText: Explain why scheduled sessions are described as 'proactive maintenance' while event-driven sessions are 'reactive responses.'
Option1: Scheduled sessions initiate work based on time (cron) regardless of whether a problem exists — they look for issues proactively. Event-driven sessions respond to something that already happened (a failure, a finding, an alert). Proactive catches problems before they escalate; reactive addresses problems after detection. Both are valuable but serve different temporal positions in the quality lifecycle
Option2: Scheduled sessions run faster because they are pre-planned
Option3: Event-driven sessions are more expensive because they run outside business hours
Option4: There is no real difference — both patterns execute the same type of work

QuestionText: A client wants weekly dependency version bumps across their 10 repositories with no human initiation. Which automation pattern applies and how should it be configured?
Option1: Scheduled session (weekly cron, e.g., every Monday at 6am) with prompt targeting dependency updates → parent spawns 10 children (one per repo) each running the dep-bump playbook → children open PRs → team reviews and merges during the week
Option2: Event-driven trigger on every npm publish event for any dependency the client uses
Option3: Manual session every Monday morning triggered by the tech lead
Option4: GitHub Dependabot exclusively — no Devin involvement needed for dependency bumps

QuestionText: A client's monitoring system fires alerts via PagerDuty webhook. They want Devin to investigate alerts automatically before waking up the on-call engineer. Configure the automation.
Option1: PagerDuty webhook → trigger layer (validate, enrich with recent logs/metrics via API call) → Devin API with prompt: 'Investigate this alert, query Datadog via MCP, identify root cause, and either open a fix PR if straightforward or post analysis to incident channel for human triage.' Safeguards: dedup by alert ID, max sessions per incident, exclude acknowledged/resolved alerts
Option2: Configure Devin to poll PagerDuty API every 30 seconds for new alerts
Option3: Give Devin access to PagerDuty's mobile app and have it respond to push notifications
Option4: Schedule a Devin session every hour that checks for recent alerts and investigates any it finds

QuestionText: A client needs an automation that fires when a PR is merged to main AND the change touches the /api/ directory, triggering an API documentation refresh session. Design this selective trigger.
Option1: GitHub webhook on push to main (or PR merged event) → trigger layer inspects the commit diff/changed files → if any file matches /api/** pattern → create Devin session with prompt: 'Update API documentation to reflect changes in the latest commit to /api/.' If no /api/ files changed → no session created. This filtering logic lives in the intermediary trigger layer, not in Devin itself
Option2: Trigger on every merge to main and include a Knowledge note telling Devin to check if /api/ changed
Option3: Schedule a daily session that checks if /api/ changed since yesterday
Option4: Configure GitHub branch protection to require documentation updates before merge

QuestionText: A client's automation should create a feedback loop: SAST scan finds issue → Devin fixes → re-scan confirms fix → if new issues introduced, Devin iterates. Design this closed-loop automation.
Option1: Initial SAST finding → Devin session applies fix → within the same session, Devin re-runs the SAST scan locally (tool installed in blueprint) → if the re-scan introduces new findings, Devin iterates on those within the same session → only opens PR when the scan is clean. This keeps the loop within a single session rather than creating cascading event triggers. The PR triggers CI-level SAST as final confirmation
Option2: SAST finding → Devin fix → PR → CI re-scans → new finding → new event → new Devin session → potentially infinite chain
Option3: SAST finding → Devin fixes all findings in one pass without re-scanning → hope for the best
Option4: SAST finding → Devin fix → human manually re-runs scan to verify → creates new ticket if issues remain

QuestionText: A client's event-driven automation creates 50+ sessions per day from CI failure events. Most sessions fail because the CI failure is a flaky test, not a real code issue. Analyze the problem.
Option1: The trigger lacks intelligence — it fires on ALL CI failures without distinguishing between real failures and flaky tests. The fix: add a filter in the trigger layer that checks test history (if the same test flipped pass/fail recently, it is likely flaky) or only triggers on failures that were not present in the previous run on the same branch. This eliminates noise sessions while still catching genuine regressions
Option2: Devin should be able to recognize flaky tests and self-terminate those sessions
Option3: Disable CI failure automation entirely — it creates too much noise
Option4: Increase the retry count so Devin eventually gets a passing run on flaky tests

QuestionText: Analyze the risk of 'automation storms': a scenario where Automation A's output triggers Automation B, which triggers Automation C, which triggers Automation A again. How does this manifest in Devin and what prevents it?
Option1: Storm scenario: Devin opens PR (event) → Devin Review triggers (automation B) → Review finds issue → remediation session opens fix PR (automation C) → fix PR triggers Devin Review again (back to B). Prevention: (1) author filtering (skip events from devin-ai-integration[bot]), (2) event deduplication (same source PR only triggers once), (3) maximum chain depth (no automation fires more than N times per source event). These must be configured explicitly by the architect — they are not automatic
Option2: Automation storms cannot happen because Devin sessions are isolated and cannot trigger each other
Option3: The platform has built-in cycle detection that automatically prevents all recursive triggers
Option4: Storms are prevented by rate limiting — if too many sessions spawn per minute, the platform queues them

QuestionText: A scheduled weekly session and an event-driven session both attempt to modify the same file simultaneously (dep bump vs. security fix, both touching package.json). Analyze the conflict.
Option1: Since each session works on its own branch and PR, both will succeed independently. The conflict surfaces when the second PR is merged after the first — standard Git merge conflict. Mitigation: Devin should rebase/update its branch if it detects a conflict before merge. Alternatively, ensure the scheduled session and event-driven session target non-overlapping scopes, or configure the event-driven session to check for in-flight scheduled PRs before starting
Option2: Sessions share a lock mechanism that prevents simultaneous access to the same file
Option3: This cannot happen because the platform serializes all sessions targeting the same repository
Option4: The second session will fail because the file is 'checked out' by the first session

QuestionText: Compare scheduled sessions (cron-based) vs. event-driven sessions for security scanning. When is each appropriate?
Option1: Scheduled: for proactive discovery of NEW vulnerabilities in existing code (scans the current state periodically). Event-driven: for reactive remediation of DETECTED findings (responds to scan results from external tools). Use both: scheduled scan → detects findings → event triggers remediation. The scan and the fix are different phases with different optimal patterns
Option2: Always use event-driven — scheduled scans are redundant if the SAST tool already runs in CI
Option3: Always use scheduled — event-driven is too reactive and misses vulnerabilities between scans
Option4: They are interchangeable — choose whichever is simpler to configure

QuestionText: A client's event-driven automation fires a Devin session when a Jira ticket is assigned to 'Devin.' The session reads the ticket and implements. But sometimes the ticket's acceptance criteria are unclear. Analyze the failure mode.
Option1: When acceptance criteria are unclear, Devin cannot verify its own work (violates 'can success be verified?' principle). The session may produce incorrect output or stall asking for clarification. Mitigation: the trigger layer should validate ticket readiness (required fields filled: acceptance criteria, expected behavior, test mechanism) before creating the session — reject incomplete tickets back to the assigner
Option2: Devin should always attempt the implementation regardless of clarity — humans will correct via PR review
Option3: The Jira workflow should prevent assignment to Devin until a human verifies the ticket is complete
Option4: Add a Knowledge note listing common acceptance criteria patterns for Devin to infer missing details

QuestionText: Analyze the cost implications of event-driven vs. scheduled patterns. A client receives ~20 SAST findings per week. Should they trigger immediately per finding or batch into a weekly remediation session?
Option1: Trade-off analysis: Immediate (20 sessions/week): faster remediation (findings fixed within hours), higher ACU cost (session overhead × 20), more PRs to review. Batched (1 session/week handling all 20): lower cost (one session startup overhead), fewer PRs (one combined PR), but findings sit exposed for up to 7 days. Optimal: CRITICAL/HIGH findings trigger immediately (risk justifies cost), MEDIUM/LOW batch weekly (acceptable exposure window). This severity-based hybrid minimizes both risk and cost
Option2: Always trigger immediately — security findings should never wait
Option3: Always batch weekly — the cost savings outweigh the risk of delayed remediation
Option4: Run one long-running session that continuously monitors for new findings and remediates in real-time

QuestionText: A client notices their scheduled maintenance sessions (running at 2am) consistently fail because the database migration from the previous night's deployment has not completed. Analyze the sequencing problem.
Option1: The scheduled session assumes a stable environment state that may not exist at the fixed time. The fix: change from time-based to event-based triggering — trigger the maintenance session after the deployment completion event (webhook from CD pipeline) rather than at a fixed time. Alternatively, add a readiness check at the start of the scheduled session that waits for the environment to be in a known-good state before proceeding
Option2: Move the scheduled session to 4am to give the migration more time to complete
Option3: Add a retry mechanism that re-runs the session every 30 minutes until it succeeds
Option4: Disable the nightly deployment and only deploy during business hours

QuestionText: Design a complete event-driven automation topology for a client's GitHub-based workflow: PR events, CI events, issue events, and deployment events should all trigger appropriate Devin responses.
Option1: PR opened: trigger Devin Review. PR comment (from human): resume existing session. CI failure on PR branch: remediation session targeting that branch. Issue labeled 'Devin:Implement': implementation session. Deployment to staging success: validation session (smoke tests). Deployment failure: investigation session. All with: author filtering (skip bot events), idempotency (one session per event), retry limits. Implemented via GitHub webhooks → serverless function → Devin API
Option2: One webhook endpoint that routes all GitHub events to a single Devin session that handles everything
Option3: GitHub Actions workflows that call the Devin API directly from within the pipeline YAML
Option4: Manual monitoring of GitHub notifications and creating sessions for important events

QuestionText: Design an anti-pattern detection system: identify when automation rules are creating excessive noise (too many low-value sessions) and automatically adjust trigger sensitivity.
Option1: (1) Track metrics: sessions created per trigger rule, session outcome (success/fail/no-output), PR merge rate per trigger. (2) Alert when: a trigger creates >X sessions/day with <Y% merge rate (noise indicator). (3) Adaptive response: auto-increase severity threshold for that trigger (e.g., from ALL findings to HIGH+CRITICAL only), or auto-pause the trigger and notify the admin. (4) Review: weekly report of trigger effectiveness — deactivate rules with consistently low value ratios. This creates a self-tuning automation system
Option2: Set fixed thresholds at configuration time and never adjust them
Option3: Rely on ACU budget limits to naturally throttle noisy automations
Option4: Have Devin sessions self-assess whether they are producing valuable output and self-terminate if not

QuestionText: Design a pattern for 'quiet hours' automation management: during release freezes or high-traffic periods, certain automations should pause while critical ones (security, incidents) continue.
Option1: Classify automations by priority tier: Tier 1 (never paused): security findings, incident response. Tier 2 (paused during freeze): dependency bumps, documentation refresh, dead code cleanup. Tier 3 (paused during high-traffic): campaigns, bulk operations. Implementation: trigger layer checks a 'freeze state' flag before invoking non-Tier-1 sessions → queues Tier 2/3 events for processing when freeze lifts. Freeze activation: manual toggle via API or scheduled (e.g., every release window)
Option2: Disable all automations during release freeze — security can wait a few days
Option3: Let all automations run during freeze — merge protection prevents accidental deploys
Option4: Reduce concurrency during freeze to 1 session at a time for all automation types

QuestionText: Design a progressive automation onboarding: a client wants to start with simple scheduled sessions and gradually add event-driven complexity. Define the progression milestones.
Option1: Month 1: Scheduled dep bumps (weekly cron, single repo, manual review). Month 2: Scheduled expanded to all repos + first event trigger (CI failure remediation for 1 repo). Month 3: Event-driven security (SAST findings → remediation). Month 4: PR events (Devin Review on all repos). Month 5: Ticket assignment trigger (Jira → implementation). Month 6: Full topology with quiet hours, severity-based routing, and campaign capability. Each step adds complexity only after the previous step is proven reliable. Rollback plan if any step produces poor results
Option2: Deploy the full event-driven topology from day 1 — there is no benefit to gradual rollout
Option3: Use only scheduled sessions forever — event-driven is too complex to manage safely
Option4: Start with the most complex automation (multi-step incident response) to prove value quickly

QuestionText: Design an automation architecture that handles cascade events: a library update in Repo A should trigger compatibility checks in Repos B, C, D that depend on it, and any failures should trigger fix sessions.
Option1: Repo A merge event → trigger layer reads dependency graph (maintained as a manifest) → spawns compatibility check sessions for repos B, C, D (each bumps A's version, runs tests) → if tests pass: open version bump PR. If tests fail: spawn fix session targeting the specific compatibility issue in that repo → fix PR opened. Chain-depth limit: the fix PR's merge does NOT trigger another cascade check (author filter). Dependency manifest is maintained as a Knowledge note or config file updated when dependencies change
Option2: Configure webhooks in repos B, C, D that fire whenever Repo A has a new commit
Option3: Weekly scheduled session that checks all dependency compatibility across all repos
Option4: No automation needed — Dependabot handles cross-repo dependency updates natively

QuestionText: Evaluate the claim: 'Event-driven automation makes scheduled sessions obsolete because everything meaningful can be triggered by an event.'
Option1: Incorrect — scheduled sessions serve a fundamentally different purpose: proactive discovery of issues that have no external event (dead code accumulation, dependency staleness, license drift, documentation decay). These are time-driven conditions, not event-triggered. A complete automation topology needs both: events for reactive response, schedules for proactive hygiene. They are complementary, not substitutes
Option2: Correct — even 'proactive' tasks can be triggered by 'time elapsed' events in a scheduler
Option3: Correct for organizations with comprehensive monitoring — any degradation triggers an alert event
Option4: Mostly correct — scheduled sessions are only needed for tasks that cannot produce events (like documentation)

QuestionText: A client has implemented 30 automation rules. After 3 months, they discover that 12 rules have never fired. Evaluate whether these rules should be removed.
Option1: Not automatically — evaluate each: some may be low-probability but high-value (incident response rules that only fire during outages). Remove rules that: (1) watch for events that the client's system never produces, (2) duplicate other rules, or (3) are outdated (target deprecated repos/tools). Keep rules that: protect against rare-but-critical scenarios (security, compliance). Refine rules that: have overly narrow filters preventing legitimate matches. Regular audits should distinguish between 'never needed' and 'not yet needed'
Option2: Remove all 12 immediately — if they have not fired in 3 months they are wasted configuration
Option3: Keep all 12 — unused rules have zero cost and provide potential future value
Option4: Replace all 12 with a single catch-all rule that fires on any unhandled event type

QuestionText: Evaluate the security implications of an event-driven system where external webhooks (from SaaS tools) can create Devin sessions that access internal code.
Option1: Security considerations: (1) Webhook authentication (verify the sender is genuine — signature validation). (2) Input validation (sanitize webhook payloads before injecting into prompts to prevent prompt injection). (3) Scoped permissions (the session created has only the access defined by its context — not elevated by the event source). (4) Rate limiting (prevent DoS via webhook flooding). (5) Audit trail (log all external triggers for security review). These controls ensure external events cannot escalate internal access
Option2: External webhooks should never trigger internal sessions — only internal events are safe
Option3: Webhook authentication alone is sufficient — if the source is verified, the session is safe
Option4: Security is not a concern because Devin sessions are already isolated by design

QuestionText: An event-driven automation architecture that triggers Devin sessions without idempotency keys is well-designed because each event is unique by nature.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: What is the recommended phased rollout approach for Devin adoption in a client organization?
Option1: Pilot (one team, limited repos) → Expand (additional teams, proven patterns) → Scale (organization-wide, full automation topology)
Option2: Training → Full deployment → Optimization
Option3: Assessment → Purchase → Launch → Support
Option4: Design → Build → Test → Deploy → Operate

QuestionText: In the unit-of-work economics model for campaigns, what is the formula for total campaign cost?
Option1: Unit cost = (ACUs per unit × $/ACU) + (Review hours per unit × Blended rate); Total = Σ(Unit cost × Quantity) + Orchestration overhead + Margin
Option2: Total cost = Number of sessions × Average session duration × Hourly rate
Option3: Total cost = Lines of code changed × Cost per line
Option4: Total cost = Number of engineers replaced × Annual salary

QuestionText: Why does the value narrative framework emphasize 'unlocking work that is currently blocked or deferred' rather than 'replacing engineers'?
Option1: Because the primary value is executing work that would otherwise NOT get done due to capacity constraints — dependency updates, security backlogs, test coverage gaps, documentation debt. Engineers are redirected to higher-value work (architecture, design, review), not eliminated. This framing is more accurate and more palatable to technical stakeholders
Option2: Because 'replacing engineers' would create negative press and reduce sales
Option3: Because Devin is not capable of replacing engineers for any task
Option4: Because labor laws prevent AI from officially replacing human jobs

QuestionText: Why is Devin's consistency described as making 'each unit more estimatable than traditional human delivery'?
Option1: When every task follows the same playbook and produces the same artifact structure, variance in execution time and cost narrows dramatically — unlike human delivery where individual productivity, context-switching, and approach differences create wide variance. This makes campaign cost estimates more reliable for SI contract bidding and sprint planning
Option2: Because AI always takes exactly the same time for every task regardless of complexity
Option3: Because Devin's ACU pricing is fixed per session regardless of output
Option4: Because the platform pre-calculates exact completion time before each session starts

QuestionText: A client has 300 SAS ETL jobs to migrate to Python/Snowflake. The migration has board-level approval and a 6-month deadline. Design the campaign planning approach.
Option1: (1) Classify jobs by complexity (simple transformations vs. complex statistical models). (2) Start with canary batch of 10 representative jobs to validate the playbook. (3) Establish success criteria per job (output parity, test coverage, scan clean). (4) Calculate unit cost from canary batch (ACUs + review hours per job). (5) Plan batches of 30-50 jobs with review capacity alignment. (6) Set escalation criteria (jobs that fail after 2 retries go to human specialists). (7) Track progress against 6-month timeline with burn-down metrics
Option2: Spawn 300 children simultaneously to meet the deadline as quickly as possible
Option3: Assign 300 jobs equally across 6 months (50/month) with no batching or validation
Option4: Complete the migration in one month using maximum parallelism, then spend 5 months on validation

QuestionText: An SI is responding to an RFP for a 50-microservice Spring Boot migration. They need to estimate cost using the Devin-augmented delivery model. How should they build the estimate?
Option1: (1) Run a pilot migration on 3 representative services to establish actual ACU cost and review time per service. (2) Categorize remaining 47 by complexity tiers (simple/medium/complex based on dependencies, custom code, and test coverage). (3) Apply observed unit costs per tier. (4) Add orchestration overhead (parent agent time, campaign management). (5) Add human specialist time for complex edge cases (estimated from pilot failure rate). (6) Include configuration investment (blueprint, playbook, Knowledge setup). (7) Apply margin. Result: bottom-up estimate with empirical basis from pilot
Option2: Estimate 1 hour per service × $100/hour ACU rate × 50 services = $5,000 total
Option3: Use the same estimate as a human-only delivery and apply a 50% discount
Option4: Quote a fixed price based on lines of code to be migrated

QuestionText: A client completed a 100-service migration campaign. 85 services migrated successfully, 10 required human intervention, and 5 were deemed unmigrateable by the agent. Analyze what this outcome tells about the engagement design.
Option1: 85% automated success is a strong result — the playbook was well-designed for the majority case. The 10% human intervention rate is expected (complex edge cases beyond playbook scope). The 5% unmigrateable represents genuinely complex work requiring human architecture decisions. This outcome validates the engagement model: use Devin for the 85% bulk work, allocate specialist time for the 15% tail. Future engagements can bid with ~85% Devin + ~15% human specialist as the cost model
Option2: 85% is too low — a good playbook should achieve 100% success without human intervention
Option3: The 5% unmigrateable services indicate a fundamental flaw in the migration approach
Option4: The engagement failed because 15% of services needed human work — Devin did not deliver full value

QuestionText: Analyze why 'start small, build trust' is recommended for cloud agent adoption rather than full-scale deployment from day one.
Option1: Starting small: (1) validates configuration in a low-risk environment, (2) builds reviewer confidence in output quality, (3) identifies playbook gaps before they affect critical systems, (4) allows the team to develop review muscle without being overwhelmed, (5) provides evidence for expanding scope. Full-scale from day one risks: overwhelming reviewers, exposing configuration gaps at scale, and eroding trust if early quality issues occur in critical repos
Option2: Starting small is only recommended for psychological comfort — there is no technical reason
Option3: Starting small is a sales strategy to get the foot in the door, not a technical recommendation
Option4: Full-scale deployment is actually better because it amortizes configuration cost faster

QuestionText: Analyze the 'competitive displacement risk' described in the value narratives. How does this apply to an SI evaluating Devin for their practice?
Option1: If a competing SI bids on the same work using a hybrid human+Devin workforce model, they can offer: lower cost (fewer human hours), shorter timeline (parallel execution), and higher consistency (playbook-driven). An SI bidding headcount-only loses on all three dimensions simultaneously. The risk is not just margin compression — it is losing deals entirely. The competitive moat comes from the SI's accumulated playbooks, configuration expertise, and campaign management capability
Option2: Competitive displacement is not a real risk — clients always prefer human-only delivery for quality
Option3: The risk only applies to commodity work like dependency bumps, not strategic migrations
Option4: Competitive displacement is a sales tactic and has no bearing on actual delivery outcomes

QuestionText: A client asks: 'How do we measure if Devin is actually delivering value?' Analyze which metrics are meaningful vs. vanity metrics.
Option1: Meaningful: PRs merged (actual delivered output), MTTR reduction (speed improvement), engineering hours freed for higher-value work (capacity gain), first-pass success rate (quality), time from trigger to PR (responsiveness). Vanity metrics: sessions created (activity ≠ value), lines of code generated (volume ≠ quality), ACUs consumed (spending ≠ impact). The key is measuring outcomes (merged work, reduced risk, faster delivery) not activity (sessions run, code generated)
Option2: Total ACU spend is the primary ROI metric — lower spend means better efficiency
Option3: Number of sessions per week is the best metric because higher volume means more value
Option4: Lines of code generated per ACU is the efficiency metric that matters most

QuestionText: Analyze the risk factors in a large-scale migration campaign and how each can be mitigated through architecture decisions.
Option1: Risks: (1) Playbook quality (mitigate: canary batch validation). (2) Target variability (mitigate: complexity classification + separate playbooks per tier). (3) Review bottleneck (mitigate: controlled concurrency + Devin Review first-pass). (4) Environment issues (mitigate: robust blueprint + pre-validation step in playbook). (5) Scope creep (mitigate: strict success criteria per unit). (6) Dependencies between targets (mitigate: identify and sequence dependent targets, parallelize only truly independent work)
Option2: The only risk is ACU cost overrun — all other risks are managed by Devin automatically
Option3: Migration campaigns have no meaningful risks because each unit is isolated and independently verifiable
Option4: The primary risk is model quality — if Devin produces bad code, the entire campaign fails

QuestionText: A client in the pilot phase reports: 'Devin works fine for simple tasks but struggles with our complex domain logic.' Analyze the likely gap and recommended next step.
Option1: The gap is likely insufficient domain context — Knowledge notes with domain glossary, architecture decisions, and coding patterns specific to their domain are missing. Devin has the technical ability but lacks the domain knowledge that human engineers carry implicitly. Next step: invest in Knowledge notes capturing domain terminology, business rules, and architectural patterns before attempting complex domain tasks. Start with research sessions ('analyze this module and explain its domain logic') to build the Knowledge base
Option2: Devin cannot handle complex domain logic — the client should limit usage to simple tasks permanently
Option3: The client needs a more powerful AI model — upgrade to a larger context window
Option4: Complex domain logic requires pair programming mode — switch from cloud to local agent for these tasks

QuestionText: Design a client engagement roadmap from initial assessment through full operational maturity. Include success criteria at each milestone.
Option1: Assessment (Week 1-2): Analyze repos, identify automation candidates, classify by complexity. Success: target repo list + complexity tiers + ROI estimate. Pilot (Week 3-6): Configure platform for 2-3 repos, run first sessions, validate output quality. Success: 5+ PRs merged, review feedback incorporated, team confidence baseline. Expand (Month 2-3): Add remaining repos, first automation, first campaign. Success: event-driven triggers functioning, campaign >80% first-pass. Scale (Month 4-6): Full automation topology, Knowledge maturity, team self-sufficient. Success: 90%+ session success rate, team can configure independently
Option2: Skip assessment — deploy immediately and learn as you go
Option3: 12-month engagement with no milestones — evaluate success only at the end
Option4: Assessment phase only — deliver a report but do not deploy anything for 6 months

QuestionText: Design a change management strategy for a 200-engineer organization adopting Devin. Address: resistance from engineers concerned about job security, managers wanting immediate ROI, and security teams demanding controls.
Option1: Engineers: frame as 'focus elevation' — Devin handles tedious work (dep bumps, boilerplate, test generation) so engineers do architecture and design. Show before/after sprint composition. Managers: set realistic 3-month timeline to ROI with progressive metrics (sessions → merged PRs → hours freed). Quick win: automate the most complained-about maintenance task. Security: walk through isolation model, demonstrate audit trail, configure initial deployment with maximum controls (all human review), relax controls as trust builds. All: transparent communication about what Devin can and cannot do
Option2: Mandate adoption from leadership with no accommodation for concerns
Option3: Address only the security team's concerns — engineers and managers will follow once it works
Option4: Delay adoption until all concerns are fully resolved through documentation and policy

QuestionText: Design a risk assessment framework for determining which client tasks are safe to automate with Devin in the first 30 days of deployment.
Option1: Score each task on 4 dimensions: (1) Verifiability (high: tests exist, scan validates; low: subjective quality). (2) Reversibility (high: branch/PR, easily reverted; low: data migration, deployed infrastructure). (3) Blast radius (high: affects production; low: test code, documentation). (4) Complexity (high: novel architecture; low: well-defined pattern). First 30 days: only automate tasks scoring 'high verifiability + high reversibility + low blast radius + low complexity.' Expand after demonstrated reliability
Option2: Automate everything immediately — the PR review gate makes all tasks safe
Option3: Only automate documentation updates in the first 30 days — all other tasks are too risky
Option4: The risk assessment depends entirely on the team's confidence level — no framework needed

QuestionText: Design a success measurement framework for a Devin deployment that addresses: engineering leadership (efficiency), security team (risk), finance (cost), and delivery managers (throughput).
Option1: Engineering leadership: % of maintenance automated, engineer time redirected to design/architecture. Security: mean-time-to-remediate findings, vulnerability exposure window, zero production incidents from agent code. Finance: ACU cost vs. equivalent contractor/FTE cost for same output, time-to-value. Delivery: PRs merged per week, campaign completion rate, time from request to delivered PR. Shared: first-pass success rate (quality across all dimensions). Report monthly by stakeholder audience with appropriate metrics per group
Option2: One dashboard showing ACU spend vs. PRs created — this covers all stakeholders
Option3: Each stakeholder group defines their own metrics independently with no coordination
Option4: Measure only 'sessions per week' as the universal proxy for all value dimensions

QuestionText: Design a playbook for an SI onboarding a new client to Devin when the client has: legacy COBOL systems, no CI/CD pipeline, manual deployments, and no test suite.
Option1: Phase 0 (Foundation): Help client establish basic CI/CD (Devin can build this as the first task — create pipeline YAML, configure runners). Phase 1 (Testing): Use Devin to generate initial test coverage for critical modules (establishes verification capability). Phase 2 (Modernization planning): Research sessions analyze COBOL codebase, produce migration assessment. Phase 3 (Pilot migration): Canary batch of simplest COBOL programs → Java, validated by new tests. Phase 4 (Scale): Campaign-based migration with proven playbook. Key: each phase creates the prerequisites for the next — you cannot migrate without tests, cannot verify without CI
Option2: Start the COBOL migration campaign immediately — Devin can handle the lack of tests
Option3: Refuse the engagement — Devin requires CI/CD and tests to be effective
Option4: Focus only on building CI/CD and tests — defer migration to a future engagement

QuestionText: Evaluate the claim: 'The timeline for this migration drops from 5 weeks to 5 days because Devin parallelizes the implementation.' Under what conditions is this realistic vs. misleading?
Option1: Realistic when: (1) units are truly independent (no cross-dependencies), (2) the playbook is proven (high first-pass success rate), (3) review capacity can absorb the parallel output, (4) CI/build infrastructure can handle the load. Misleading when: (1) review becomes the bottleneck (10 PRs/day capacity → still 5 days even with instant implementation), (2) targets have dependencies requiring sequencing, (3) the playbook needs iteration (canary batch time adds days), (4) infrastructure cannot handle 50 concurrent builds. The claim is aspirational ceiling, not guaranteed outcome
Option2: Always realistic — parallel execution fundamentally eliminates timeline constraints
Option3: Always misleading — AI cannot match the quality of 5 weeks of human engineering in 5 days
Option4: Realistic only for trivial tasks like dependency bumps, not for real migrations

QuestionText: Evaluate the operating model where an SI delivers a migration project with 80% Devin-executed work and 20% human specialist work. What are the margin implications vs. a 100% human model?
Option1: The hybrid model improves margins because: ACU cost per unit < equivalent human labor cost per unit, throughput is higher (parallel execution compresses timeline), and the human 20% focuses on high-judgment work where the SI's expertise differentiates. Risks: if playbook quality is poor, rework erodes margins. Key metric: first-pass success rate directly correlates with margin — higher success = less rework = better economics. As playbooks mature over engagements, margins improve over time (the compounding advantage)
Option2: Margins are worse because ACU costs plus human costs exceed pure human costs
Option3: Margins are identical because the same total output requires the same total cost regardless of who executes it
Option4: The hybrid model only improves margins for large engagements (100+ units) but hurts small ones

QuestionText: A client asks: 'What if Devin breaks something in production?' Evaluate the risk and explain why the architecture prevents this.
Option1: Devin CANNOT directly break production because: (1) it works on branches, never main directly, (2) PRs require human approval to merge, (3) CI gates must pass before merge is allowed, (4) deployment to production is handled by CD pipelines, not Devin — Devin does not deploy. The risk of production breakage is reduced to the same risk of any code change — a human approved it, CI validated it, and the deployment pipeline executed it. Devin's contribution is the PR; the production decision chain is unchanged
Option2: Devin could break production if it has deployment credentials — the risk is real and unmitigated
Option3: The risk is zero because Devin's AI is trained to never produce code with bugs
Option4: The risk exists but is acceptabl because Devin can also fix production issues it causes

QuestionText: Evaluate whether Devin is appropriate for a client with: 2 developers, 3 repositories, no automation, and very limited budget. What is the minimum viable adoption?
Option1: Minimum viable adoption: (1) Connect 1-2 repos, (2) basic blueprint for build/test, (3) 2-3 Knowledge notes for key conventions. Use for: scheduled dep bumps (offload maintenance from the 2 devs), test generation (improve coverage without allocating dev time), and manual sessions for implementation work. ROI depends on session volume — at low volume, per-session quality is less critical than at scale. Value proposition: the 2 developers spend less time on maintenance and more on features. Budget consideration: ACU-based billing means cost scales with usage, not seats
Option2: Not appropriate — Devin requires minimum 10 engineers and 20 repos to be cost-effective
Option3: Deploy the full automation topology — small teams benefit most from maximum automation
Option4: Only use Devin CLI (local agent) — cloud Devin is not worth it for 2 developers

QuestionText: Evaluate the engagement approach where an SI builds Devin configuration as a deliverable: 'We configure your org, create playbooks, and hand over the keys.' Is this sufficient?
Option1: Configuration-as-deliverable is necessary but not sufficient. Missing: (1) team enablement (engineers must learn to write prompts, review output, and refine playbooks), (2) continuous improvement handoff (team must own the Knowledge/playbook evolution), (3) operational runbook (monitoring automation health, handling failures). A complete engagement includes: configure + enable + validate + hand off operations. Without enablement, the configuration atrophies as the codebase evolves and no one maintains it
Option2: This is fully sufficient — once configured, Devin runs autonomously forever without maintenance
Option3: The SI should maintain the Devin configuration permanently as a managed service
Option4: Configuration is unnecessary — just train the team to write good prompts

QuestionText: Evaluate the viability of a 'Devin-first' development model where the default path for any new task is to assign it to Devin, falling back to humans only when Devin cannot complete it.
Option1: Viable for well-defined tasks that fit the 'sweet spot' criteria (verifiable, repetitive, lower priority, automatable, safe to iterate). NOT viable as a universal default because: novel architecture decisions, creative design work, politically sensitive changes, and ambiguous requirements all belong to humans from the start. The optimal model is: classify tasks first (AI-suitable vs. human-required), then route appropriately. 'Try Devin first for everything' wastes ACUs on tasks that will inevitably need human restart
Option2: Fully viable — Devin should attempt every task and humans clean up what it cannot do
Option3: Not viable — most tasks require human judgment that Devin cannot provide
Option4: Viable only if the team has spent 6+ months building up the Knowledge and playbook library

QuestionText: Evaluate the long-term sustainability of Devin campaigns as the client's codebase modernizes. Do campaigns become less valuable over time?
Option1: Campaigns evolve rather than diminish: initial value is modernization (legacy → modern). After modernization: campaigns shift to ongoing maintenance (quarterly upgrades, security remediation, compliance sweeps), expansion (new features deployed across services simultaneously), and continuous improvement (progressive quality improvements). The campaign pattern remains valuable — the targets change from 'migration units' to 'maintenance units.' Organizations that maintain their Devin investment find new campaign opportunities as their needs evolve
Option2: Campaigns become obsolete after the initial migration is complete — they are a one-time tool
Option3: Campaigns become MORE valuable over time because there are more services to maintain
Option4: After modernization, the organization should switch from campaigns to purely event-driven patterns

QuestionText: A pilot deployment that demonstrates ROI on low-risk tasks (documentation, dependency bumps) is sufficient evidence to justify organization-wide deployment for complex migration campaigns.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: What is Devin's escalation behavior when it cannot resolve a CI failure after multiple attempts?
Option1: Devin asks the user for help rather than looping indefinitely — it recognizes when it is stuck and escalates to human judgment
Option2: Devin force-merges the PR to skip the failing check
Option3: Devin deletes the branch and starts over from scratch
Option4: Devin continues retrying infinitely until the check passes

QuestionText: In the parent-child pattern, a failure in one child agent does NOT affect the execution of other child agents because they run on separate isolated VMs.
Option1: True
Option2: False

QuestionText: Why is Devin's iterate-then-escalate behavior architecturally important for autonomous operations?
Option1: It prevents two failure modes: (1) giving up too early (missing solvable problems that need a second attempt), and (2) looping forever (wasting ACUs on unsolvable problems). The bounded retry with escalation balances autonomy with practical resource management and ensures humans are notified only when their input is actually needed
Option2: It is required for compliance because humans must always be in the loop
Option3: It reduces ACU cost by limiting the number of retries per session
Option4: It prevents Devin from overwriting its previous work by iterating too many times

QuestionText: A campaign of 80 children has 20 failures. The parent must decide how to handle them. 15 failures share the same error (missing credential), 5 have unique errors. Design the recovery approach.
Option1: Systematic failures (15): fix the root cause (add the missing credential to secrets/blueprint), then retry all 15 as a batch. Idiosyncratic failures (5): analyze each individually — retry with modified parameters for those that might succeed, escalate to humans for those requiring judgment. Do NOT retry without fixing the systematic issue — it would produce 15 more identical failures
Option2: Retry all 20 failures immediately — some may succeed on the second attempt
Option3: Mark all 20 as permanent failures and report to the team for manual implementation
Option4: Restart the entire 80-child campaign from scratch with updated configuration

QuestionText: An event-driven automation fires during a Devin platform outage. The webhook is received but sessions cannot be created. Design the recovery mechanism.
Option1: The trigger layer should implement: (1) Dead letter queue for failed session creation attempts. (2) Retry with exponential backoff when the API returns errors. (3) Alerting when the queue depth exceeds a threshold (platform may be down). (4) Replay capability when the platform recovers — drain the queue and create sessions for queued events. (5) Idempotency ensures replayed events do not create duplicate sessions if some originally succeeded. This ensures no events are lost during transient platform issues
Option2: Accept event loss during outages — the events will recur naturally and be caught next time
Option3: Send failed events via email to the engineering team for manual session creation
Option4: Configure redundant Devin platform instances for high availability

QuestionText: Analyze the difference between a 'retryable failure' and a 'non-retryable failure' in the context of Devin campaigns. Provide examples of each.
Option1: Retryable: transient network timeout, flaky test (may pass on retry), temporary rate limit, package registry momentary unavailability — the same inputs might produce different results next time. Non-retryable: missing credential (systematic — will fail again identically), incompatible code change (logical — requires approach modification), deprecated API with no equivalent (architectural — requires human decision). The key distinction: will retrying with identical inputs potentially succeed (retryable) or definitely fail again (non-retryable)?
Option2: All failures are retryable if you retry enough times — persistence always works
Option3: Network errors are retryable; code errors are non-retryable — it is purely about the error type
Option4: Whether a failure is retryable depends on how many ACUs remain in the session budget

QuestionText: A campaign produces 50 PRs but 10 fail CI after merge (integration test failures in the target branch). Analyze the failure pattern and identify the architectural gap.
Option1: Post-merge CI failures indicate that individual child PRs passed in isolation but create conflicts or incompatibilities when combined. The architectural gap: the campaign lacked integration validation between children's changes. Mitigation: for campaigns that modify shared systems, add a post-merge integration test phase — if failures occur, spawn a fix session targeting the integrated state. For future campaigns, consider whether children's changes can interact and add cross-child validation steps
Option2: This is normal — integration failures after merge are the human team's responsibility, not the campaign's
Option3: Each child should have run the full integration test suite against all other children's changes before creating its PR
Option4: The CI pipeline is unreliable — post-merge failures are always infrastructure issues

QuestionText: Analyze the 'circuit breaker' pattern for automation: when should the automation system stop triggering new sessions and why?
Option1: Trip the circuit breaker when: (1) failure rate exceeds threshold (e.g., >50% of last N sessions failed — indicates systematic issue). (2) A single event type produces consecutive failures (the event source may be sending malformed data). (3) ACU spend rate exceeds budget velocity (runaway sessions). Why: continuing to create sessions that will fail wastes resources, may create noise (failed PRs, partial changes), and overwhelms the human escalation channel. The breaker allows recovery: fix the root cause, reset the breaker, and resume
Option2: Never — automations should always attempt to handle events even during high failure rates
Option3: Only when the ACU budget is fully exhausted for the billing period
Option4: When a human administrator manually disables the automation after noticing problems

QuestionText: A session opened a PR, CI passed, a reviewer approved, but the merge failed due to a merge conflict with another PR that was merged first. Analyze Devin's expected recovery behavior.
Option1: Devin should detect the merge conflict notification, resume from hibernation, rebase its branch on the updated main (resolving conflicts), push the updated branch, wait for CI to re-run, and request re-review if the conflict resolution changed the diff significantly. This is normal git workflow — merge conflicts are an expected operational condition, not an error
Option2: Devin should close the PR and open a new one since the original is now stale
Option3: Devin cannot handle merge conflicts — it must escalate to a human engineer
Option4: The reviewer should merge manually using the 'resolve conflicts' button in the Git UI

QuestionText: Analyze what happens to in-flight Devin sessions during a scheduled secret rotation. If a session is actively using a credential when it is rotated, what is the expected behavior?
Option1: The active session continues using the credential that was injected at session start — rotation changes the stored value for FUTURE sessions but does not revoke the in-flight session's already-injected copy. The session completes normally. Only the next session startup will receive the new credential value. This is by design — you cannot disrupt active sessions with rotation. If immediate revocation is needed (security incident), revoke at the credential provider level (e.g., invalidate the API key in the vendor's console), which affects all uses including in-flight
Option2: The session immediately fails when the credential is rotated mid-execution
Option3: The platform pauses the session, injects the new credential, and resumes execution
Option4: Secret rotation is blocked while any session is actively using the credential

QuestionText: Design a graceful degradation strategy for a client's automation topology when partial platform failures occur: e.g., MCP server down, scheduled sessions delayed, or event queue backed up.
Option1: MCP down: sessions continue core work (code, test, PR) without the integration — skip MCP-dependent steps (Jira status update, Datadog query) and flag incomplete items for human follow-up. Scheduled sessions delayed: dead letter queue with automatic replay when service recovers + alerting on SLA breach. Event queue backed up: process events in priority order (security > incidents > maintenance), apply circuit breaker on low-priority triggers to prevent cascade failure. All failures logged with clear diagnostics for post-incident review. Key: no single integration failure should halt all automation
Option2: If any component fails, halt all automation until the issue is fully resolved
Option3: Ignore partial failures — sessions will retry automatically and eventually succeed
Option4: Switch to manual operation mode during any platform instability

QuestionText: Design a rollback strategy for a campaign where post-merge analysis reveals a systematic flaw: all 30 merged PRs introduced a subtle performance regression. How should the architect handle this?
Option1: (1) Immediately pause any remaining unmerged campaign PRs. (2) Assess impact: is the regression severe enough to warrant rollback or acceptable until fixed forward? (3) If rollback needed: spawn a rollback campaign — parent creates revert PRs for all 30 merges (children revert each commit, run tests). (4) Identify root cause in playbook that caused the regression. (5) Fix the playbook. (6) Re-run the campaign with corrected methodology on the reverted codebase. (7) Add regression detection to the playbook's verification steps for future campaigns. Post-mortem: why did CI not catch the performance issue? Add performance tests to the pipeline
Option2: git revert all 30 commits manually and redo the entire campaign from scratch
Option3: Fix forward — deploy a single fix that addresses the regression without reverting the 30 PRs
Option4: Rollback is not possible for merged code — accept the regression and fix it gradually

QuestionText: Evaluate the claim: 'A well-designed Devin automation should never fail.' Is this a reasonable engineering standard?
Option1: This is unreasonable and reveals a misunderstanding of distributed systems. Failures are inevitable: network issues, dependency changes, edge cases in code, concurrent modifications. The correct standard is: automation should fail gracefully (detect, classify, escalate), recover automatically from transient issues, and provide clear diagnostics for permanent failures. 'Design for failure' is superior to 'prevent all failure.' The maturity metric is mean-time-to-recovery, not failure count = 0
Option2: This is reasonable — with enough testing and configuration, zero failures is achievable
Option3: This is reasonable for scheduled sessions but not for event-driven ones
Option4: This is unreasonable only because AI models are inherently unreliable — better models would make it achievable

QuestionText: Evaluate the following escalation policy: 'Devin should retry 10 times before escalating to a human.' Is 10 an appropriate retry count?
Option1: 10 retries is almost certainly too many for most failure types. For non-transient failures (missing credentials, logical errors, incompatible APIs), retrying wastes ACUs — the failure will recur identically. For transient failures (network timeouts), 2-3 retries with backoff is sufficient. Best practice: 2-3 retries for transient errors, immediate escalation for non-transient errors (detect from error type). The retry count should depend on failure classification, not a universal constant
Option2: 10 retries is reasonable — it gives Devin enough attempts to find alternative approaches
Option3: 10 retries is too few — Devin should try at least 20 times before bothering a human
Option4: Retry count does not matter because ACU cost is negligible compared to human intervention cost

QuestionText: A campaign of 100 children completed with: 70 success, 20 retried-then-succeeded, 8 escalated to humans, 2 permanently failed. Evaluate this outcome distribution.
Option1: This is a healthy distribution: 70% first-pass success (good playbook quality), 20% self-healed through retry (system resilience working), 8% correctly escalated (humans handle genuine complexity), 2% permanent failure (acceptable — complex edge cases exist). Improvement opportunities: analyze the 20 retries to see if their initial failures could be prevented (Knowledge notes, better blueprint), and examine the 8 escalations for patterns that could be added to the playbook. Overall: the system is working as designed
Option2: 70% first-pass is too low — the playbook needs significant improvement before running more campaigns
Option3: The 2% permanent failures represent an unacceptable engineering standard — the playbook must handle 100%
Option4: The 20% retry rate indicates infrastructure instability that should be investigated immediately

QuestionText: Evaluate whether a Devin automation should automatically rollback its own changes if post-merge monitoring detects an issue. What are the risks?
Option1: Auto-rollback introduces significant risks: (1) false positive detection could revert valid changes, (2) the revert itself could have side effects (dependent code may have already been built on the original change), (3) it bypasses the human review gate (auto-revert means no human approves the revert). Better approach: auto-detect and alert → human decides whether to revert → if approved, Devin executes the revert (agent implements, human authorizes). This maintains the governance principle that humans make release decisions
Option2: Auto-rollback is always safe because reverting to a known-good state cannot introduce new issues
Option3: Auto-rollback is appropriate and should be the default for all automated changes
Option4: Rollback should never be automated — even the detection should be manual

QuestionText: Evaluate the resilience of a campaign architecture where the parent agent is the single point of failure — if the parent session crashes, all campaign state is lost.
Option1: This is a real architectural weakness. Mitigations: (1) Parent persists campaign state externally (GitHub issue, tracking document) so it can be reconstructed. (2) Children are independent — parent crash does not stop running children. (3) A new parent session can be created that reads the persisted state and resumes monitoring. (4) Progressive state checkpoints (parent writes status after each batch launch). The key insight: children's execution is independent of parent health — only monitoring/coordination is lost, not the work itself
Option2: This is not a real risk because Devin sessions never crash — the platform guarantees availability
Option3: This is acceptable — simply restart the entire campaign if the parent crashes
Option4: This makes campaigns unsuitable for production use — only use single-agent patterns

QuestionText: Evaluate the following recovery strategy: 'When a scheduled maintenance session fails, we wait until the next scheduled run rather than immediate retry.' Is this acceptable?
Option1: Depends on the task's time-sensitivity and failure mode. Acceptable for: non-urgent tasks where waiting a week is fine (dead code cleanup, docs refresh).
