NOT acceptable for: security-related maintenance (vulnerability scanning), time-sensitive compliance checks, or when the failure indicates a systematic issue that will recur. The strategy should classify failures: transient (retry soon, within the schedule window), systematic (fix root cause before next run), and time-sensitive (retry immediately regardless of schedule)
Option2: Always acceptable — the schedule exists for a reason and should be respected
Option3: Never acceptable — all failures should trigger immediate retry
Option4: Acceptable only if the scheduled interval is less than 24 hours

QuestionText: A client's automation topology has no monitoring or alerting — failures silently accumulate. Evaluate the operational risk and prescribed minimum monitoring.
Option1: Critical operational risk — silent failures mean: (1) security findings sit unremediated, (2) scheduled maintenance quietly stops working, (3) event-driven responses silently fail to fire, (4) the team assumes automation is working when it is not. Minimum monitoring: (1) heartbeat check that scheduled sessions are actually running on schedule, (2) failure rate tracking per automation rule, (3) alerts when any trigger fails to create a session, (4) aggregate dashboard showing automation health. Without this, the team cannot distinguish 'working' from 'silently broken'
Option2: No monitoring needed — if automation fails, the team will notice when the work does not get done
Option3: Monitoring is only needed for security automations — other failures are low-risk
Option4: Platform provides all necessary monitoring automatically — no additional setup required

QuestionText: Evaluate the principle: 'The merge decision always belongs to a human' as an exception handling mechanism. Under what extreme circumstances might this principle be relaxed?
Option1: Potentially acceptable relaxation for: (1) auto-generated test-only PRs that cannot affect production behavior, (2) automated dependency bumps where all tests pass (low blast radius + full verification), (3) documentation-only changes, (4) when the change is a revert of a known-bad commit. Even in these cases: auto-merge should only apply to specific file patterns with full CI passing, and a human should be able to disable auto-merge at any time. The principle should be the DEFAULT that is relaxed deliberately and narrowly, never the exception that is enforced selectively
Option2: Never relaxed under any circumstances — all changes require human approval
Option3: Relaxed whenever CI passes — CI is sufficient verification for all change types
Option4: Relaxed after a team reaches 'Strategic trust' maturity level for all automation output

QuestionText: Explain what the principle "sessions are ephemeral; knowledge is persistent" means in the context of Devin's architecture.
Option1: Each session's VM is temporary and destroyed after use (ephemeral), but the organization's accumulated configuration — blueprints, Knowledge notes, Playbooks, Secrets, MCP servers, Git connections — persists in the shared context layer and flows into every future session automatically
Option2: Sessions are deleted after 24 hours but Knowledge notes are stored forever
Option3: Session code is temporary but the AI model retains memory of past sessions
Option4: The VM persists but the agent's conversation history is erased between sessions

QuestionText: A client observes that Devin's local test results pass but CI fails on the same code. Analyze the architectural explanation for this discrepancy.
Option1: The VM's snapshot-based environment may differ from the CI runner's environment in OS version, dependency versions, or test data — the blueprint should mirror CI's environment as closely as possible to minimize drift
Option2: This is impossible — if local tests pass on Devin's VM, CI must also pass since they execute identical code
Option3: Devin always runs tests with newer package versions than CI, so the fix is to downgrade packages on the VM
Option4: This occurs because Devin runs tests in parallel while CI runs them sequentially, creating race conditions

QuestionText: Compare the architectural implications of org-scoped secrets versus repo-scoped secrets versus user-scoped secrets. When is each scope appropriate?
Option1: Org-scoped: shared service accounts used across all repos (e.g., Jira API token). Repo-scoped: credentials specific to one repository's infrastructure (e.g., deploy key). User-scoped: personal credentials not shared with others (e.g., personal PAT). The hierarchy prevents over-sharing while enabling appropriate access at each level
Option2: All secrets should be org-scoped for simplicity — team members all work on the same systems anyway
Option3: Repo-scoped secrets are always preferable because they follow the principle of least privilege for every case
Option4: User-scoped secrets should never be used because Devin is a team resource, not a personal tool

QuestionText: Design a verification strategy for a client whose application has: fast unit tests (2 minutes), slow integration tests (30 minutes), and external contract tests requiring a staging environment. Map each to the appropriate verification tier in Devin's model.
Option1: Local VM: run unit tests (2 min) for fast feedback before pushing. CI pipeline: run integration tests (30 min) after push — Devin monitors and iterates on failures. External validation: contract tests triggered after CI passes — Devin monitors the final gate. Each tier provides increasingly rigorous verification while keeping the inner development loop fast
Option2: Run all three test tiers locally on the VM to minimize total feedback loop time, even if it takes 30+ minutes per iteration
Option3: Skip local testing entirely and push immediately — let CI handle all verification to avoid environment drift
Option4: Only run unit tests via Devin — integration and contract testing are manual human-only activities

QuestionText: A client reports that their Devin sessions take 12 minutes to install dependencies before starting actual coding work. The repository has a large Python dependency tree. How should the architect optimize session startup?
Option1: Move the pip install and dependency setup into the blueprint's initialize block so dependencies are baked into the VM snapshot — sessions boot with everything pre-installed, eliminating the 12-minute install on every session start
Option2: Configure Devin to use a faster internet connection for PyPI downloads
Option3: Switch from pip to poetry because it resolves dependencies faster
Option4: Add the virtual environment directory to the Git repository so it is cloned with the code

QuestionText: Why does the SDLC integration model specify explicit human touchpoints at each phase rather than allowing fully autonomous end-to-end execution?
Option1: Human touchpoints provide judgment at decision points that require context the agent cannot access — design trade-offs, business priority, organizational politics, and risk tolerance. The agent handles implementation labor between these decision points efficiently
Option2: Fully autonomous execution is technically impossible with current AI capabilities
Option3: Human touchpoints are required by law for all software development processes
Option4: The platform is designed to require human input at fixed intervals regardless of the task

QuestionText: Map the five SDLC phases to their appropriate Devin interaction patterns: Planning, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Operations.
Option1: Planning: Ask Devin for research/analysis. Implementation: Cloud session for autonomous coding. Testing: Parallel sessions for test generation and coverage. Deployment: IaC changes via PR with human approval gate. Operations: Scheduled + event-driven sessions for maintenance and incident response
Option2: All five phases use the same cloud session pattern — create a session and let Devin handle everything
Option3: Planning and Deployment are human-only; Devin only participates in Implementation and Testing
Option4: Operations should not involve Devin because production systems require human-only management

QuestionText: Compare event-driven SAST remediation with scheduled SAST remediation. What are the trade-offs?
Option1: Event-driven reacts immediately to findings (faster remediation, shorter exposure window) but requires webhook infrastructure and may create session volume spikes. Scheduled runs at fixed intervals (simpler setup, predictable cost) but leaves findings open between scan intervals. Optimal: trigger immediately for CRITICAL/HIGH severity, batch MEDIUM/LOW into scheduled runs
Option2: Event-driven is strictly superior in all cases — there is no reason to use scheduled scanning
Option3: Scheduled is strictly superior because it provides predictable cost and workload
Option4: There is no practical difference between the two approaches for security outcomes

QuestionText: Design a trigger chain for a ticket-to-staging workflow: a Jira ticket is assigned for implementation, Devin implements, CI validates, and the merged code is deployed to staging for acceptance testing.
Option1: Jira webhook (ticket assigned to Devin label) → trigger layer creates Devin session with ticket context → Devin implements and opens PR → CI runs tests + SAST → human reviews and merges → CD pipeline deploys to staging → event-driven Devin session runs smoke tests against staging endpoint and reports results back to Jira ticket
Option2: Jira webhook → Devin implements and deploys directly to staging in one session → human reviews in staging
Option3: Scheduled nightly session reads all new tickets, implements in batch, and auto-merges if CI passes
Option4: Human manually creates a Devin session for each ticket, then manually deploys after review

QuestionText: Evaluate the following proposal: "Devin Review should replace one of the two required human reviewers on all PRs to speed up the review process." What is the strongest argument against this?
Option1: Devin Review excels at detecting logic errors, security issues, and style violations but cannot evaluate architectural fit, business context, or team-specific design philosophy — replacing a human reviewer removes a qualitative judgment layer that automated analysis cannot replicate
Option2: Devin Review is too slow and would create a bottleneck in the merge process
Option3: Regulatory requirements always mandate two human reviewers regardless of automated tooling
Option4: Devin Review would approve too many PRs, reducing the team's overall code quality standards
